Correction

Article title: La Práctica del Psicoanálisis. Evolución y actualidad by Juan Pablo Jiménez and Josefina Figueroa Cox

Authors: Vinocur, Susana.

Journal: The International Journal of Psychoanalysis

Bibliometrics: Volume 102, Number 5, pages 1055 - 1059

DOI: https://doi.org/ 10.1080/00207578.2021.1923371

When the above article first published online, the following errors were present.

Two reference entries were missing:

de León de Bernardi, B. 1999. Un modo de pensar la clínica: vigencia y perspectivas del enfoque de W. y M. Baranger 47-7. En Volviendo a pensar en Willy y Madeleine Baranger Editorial: LUMEN, Buenos Aires.

de León de Bernardi, B. 2008. Introduction to the paper by Madeleine and Willy Baranger: The analytic situation as a dynamic field. Int. J. Psychoanal. 89:773–784. Published also in: Revista Uruguaya de Psicoanálisis. n. 108: 198-222, 2009; and in The Turkish Annual Book.

The following citation was incorrect:

Among the classics, however, there is no mention of the work of Berenstein and Puget ([1967] 1997), who marked a milestone in Latin American ideas about the setting in science and psychoanalysis from a methodological perspective inspired by – but also expanded from – the work of Susan Isaacs.

responseSet.title: Statement of Retraction: Class and Psychoanalysis: Landscapes of Inequality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Statement of Retraction: Class and Psychoanalysis: Landscapes of Inequality

We, the Editor and Publishers of The International Journal of Psychoanalysis, have jointly agreed to retract the following Book Review:

David Morgan (2020) Class and psychoanalysis: landscapes of inequality, The International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 101:2, 418-422, DOI: 10.1080/00207578.2019.1651203

This article contains extensive content similarity with the previously published Book Review Essay:

David Morgan (2019) Class and psychoanalysis: landscapes of inequality, Psychodynamic Practice, 25:3, 293-299, DOI: 10.1080/14753634.2018.1564885

The previous publication was not referenced or acknowledged.

We would like to acknowledge that the author has been fully cooperative during our investigation.

We have been informed in our decision-making by our policy on publishing ethics and integrity and the COPE guidelines on retractions.

The retracted article will remain online to maintain the scholarly record, but it will be digitally watermarked on each page as “Retracted”.

833

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ce que le nazisme a fait à la psychanalyse (What Nazism did to psychoanalysis)

Book authors: Laurence Kahn, ISBN: 978-2-13-080380-5, Price Book: €14, Publisher: PUF, Publisher Location: Paris, Year of Publication: 2018

Mathilde Girard

Mathilde Girard

Mathilde Girard   mathilde.girard@ymail.com

What Nazism Did to Psychoanalysis is the latest book by Laurence Kahn, a member of the Psychoanalytic Association of France (APF), which she chaired from 2008 to 2010. She is the author of several recent books: Cures d’enfance (Childhood Cures) (2004), Faire parler le destin (Making Destiny Speak) (2005), L’Écoute de l’analyste (The Analyst’s Attentiveness) (2012) and Le psychanalyste apathique et le patient postmoderne (The Apathetic Psychoanalyst and the Postmodern Patient) (2014).

To know my present, I look to my past.

Where does the present of psychoanalysis come from? And from which sources? We no longer study sufficient history in psychoanalysis, and we struggle to pinpoint ourselves in today’s realities. We generally also do not link the daily life of our practices, the concepts with which we work, to their origins and when they were first invented. We sometimes move forward without questioning the genealogy of our concepts, and soon we apply them without understanding; we repeat them without questioning.

Against this kind of defect, Laurence Kahn’s position is always rigorous, and acts as an antidote to theoretical deviations. Her aim in this book is to return to psychoanalysis as a movement crisscrossed by crises, especially those of contemporary history; her most recent titles – Le psychanalyste apathique et le patient postmoderne (The Apathetic Psychoanalyst and the Postmodern Patient) and Faire parler le destin (Making Destiny Speak) – have this aim too. Psychoanalysis belongs to the twentieth century and is tied to its destiny. We have not yet evaluated the consequences of this link, precisely because history and its writing are made of 212

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Bruce Reis

Bruce Reis

New York City, USA
bruce.reis@nyu.edu

Bruce Reis  

I would like to thank Dr Nancy Chodorow for her letter. I understand that Dr Chodorow is displeased with my choice of Dr Paula Ellman to review her book The Psychoanalytic Ear and the Sociological Eye: Toward an American Independent Tradition in these pages and with Dr Ellman's review. However, I take issue with some of Dr Chodorow's claims and hope my addressing them will lead to her greater satisfaction.

First, while it is true that Dr Ellman is the current Chair of the IPA Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis, she is not only that. Dr Ellman is also Faculty and a Training and Supervising Analyst at both the Contemporary Freudian Society and the Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis (APsaA), and a long-serving member of the Board of the Journal. Thus, I take issue with Dr Chodorow's opening question that seems to suggest Dr Ellman is in some way not up to the task of reviewing her book and was selected only because of her expertise in the area of women and psychoanalysis. I believe it would do Dr Ellman a disservice to suggest that she is not enough familiar with the work of Loewald, Erikson, ego psychologists, intersubjectivists or Dr Chodorow herself to review her book, and myself a disservice to suggest that I “pigeonholed” the author by selecting Dr Ellman when she is in fact a strong theorist and writer in her own right, not only regarding women's issues.

Second, it has never been a requirement, as Dr Chodorow knows having herself served as North American Book Review Editor (NA BRE), that a reviewer for a book need to have published in the area of the book's subject; indeed creating such a requirement would greatly limit the number of potential reviewers of books. Finding an appropriate reviewer for an author such as Dr Chodorow, whose career has been both distinguished and long lasting, is challenging as one cannot simply ask the colleagues with whom she developed the American middle school approach since these are intimates with whom she has shared professional relationships for decades, and creating a rule such as that has potential to result in logrolling among friends rather than critical evaluation.

responseSet.title: “Operational” concepts in the phenomenon of Islamist radicalization: From the subject to the apocalypse
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Operational” concepts in the phenomenon of Islamist radicalization: From the subject to the apocalypse

Annabelle Jaccard and Giorgia Tiscini

Giorgia Tiscini

Paris
France

Rennes
France

Giorgia Tiscini   CONTACT Giorgia Tiscini giorgia.tiscini@univ-rennes2.fr
Hatred, melancholy, drive, symptom, radicalization, haine, mélancolie, pulsion, symptôme, radicalisation

Taken from interviews with men having been incarcerated on the charge of criminal association with the intent of committing or aiding and abetting to terrorist acts, the central question of this article has thus emerged: which part does Islamist radicalization play on the psychic level of the self? Therefore, we have selected several theorical concepts, to guide us in this research, that became operative concepts, such as hatred, drive, melancholy and symptom. These have a converging point that is linked with the clinic of boredom and emptiness, or named otherwise, “off language”. The radicalization process seems to come as a way to fill this emptiness, and hence to avoid the risk of the collapsing of the self. However, this “solution” is only a lure, leading to destruction, while at the same time, the person is aiming to restore its identity.

“Operational” concepts in the phenomenon of Islamist radicalization: from the subject to the apocalypse. Based on interviews with men who have been incarcerated for the intention to commit or for aiding and abetting terrorist acts, the central question of this article is: what part does Islamic radicalization play on a psychic level? Drawing on concepts such as hatred, drive, and melancholy, the paper looks at how these are connected to boredom and emptiness - or “off language”. The radicalization process is a way of filling this emptiness and of avoiding the risk of the collapse of self. However, while the person aims to restore a sense of identity, the “solution” is a lure, leading only to destruction.

Concepts opératoires dans le phénomène de la radicalisation islamiste: du sujet à l'apocalypse. C'est à partir d'une recherche avec des hommes incarcérés pour association de malfaiteurs en relation avec une entreprise terroriste, appuyée sur une méthodologie clinique d'orientation psychanalytique et effectuée en milieu carcéral, que le questionnement central de cet article a émergé : quelle place occupe la radicalisation islamiste dans l'économie psychique du sujet ? Nous avons ainsi isolé des concepts théoriques qui sont devenus des concepts opératoires, à savoir la haine, la pulsion, la mélancolie et le symptôme. Le point de croisement de ces derniers serait le vide, autrement désigné ennui, blanc ou hors-langage. Le processus de radicalisation semble pouvoir combler ce vide, afin de contrer un risque d'effondrement subjectif. Toutefois, cette « solution » n'est qu'un leurre amenant à la destruction, tout en cherchant à réhabiliter le narcissisme et la recherche d'identification.

„Operationale“ Konzepte zum Phänomen islamistischer Radikalisierung: vom Subjekt zur Apokalypse. Aus Interviews mit Männern, die wegen ihrer Absicht, eine terroristische Straftat zu begehen oder wegen Beihilfe zu terroristischen Handlungen inhaftiert wurden, ergab sich die zentrale Frage dieses Beitrags: Welche Rolle spielt die islamische Radikalisierung auf der psychischen Ebene? Aus Konzepten wie Hass, Trieb und Melancholie schöpfend untersucht dieser Beitrag, wie diese mit Gefühlen von Langeweile und Leere – oder dem „Außersprachlichen“ – in Verbindung stehen. Der Radikalisierungsprozess ist ein Weg, um diese Leere zu füllen und das Selbst vor dem Zusammenbruch zu bewahren. Während die Person jedoch ein Identitätsbewusstsein wiederherstellen möchte, ist die „Lösung“ nur ein täuschendes Lockmittel, das unweigerlich in die Zerstörung führt.

Concetti operativi nel fenomeno della radicalizzazione islamica: dal soggetto all'apocalisse. È a partire da una ricerca condotta con uomini incarcerati per associazione a delinquere in relazione ad atti di natura terroristica - una ricerca impostata su una metodologia clinica di orientamento psicoanalitico ed effettuata in ambiente carcerario - che è man mano emersa la domanda centrale del presente lavoro: quale posto occupa la radicalizzazione islamica all'interno dell'economia psichica del soggetto? Dopo avere individuato una serie di concetti teorici da utilizzare come concetti operativi (ad es. odio, pulsione, melanconia e sintomo) abbiamo riconosciuto come nesso che li collega tutti il vuoto: un'entità designabile anche con i termini di noia o “fuori-dal-linguaggio”. Il processo di radicalizzazione rappresenta una strategia per colmare questo vuoto ed evitare il rischio di un collasso del soggetto: vero è però che, per quanto la persona miri a ricostituire in tal modo il proprio narcisismo e un senso di identità, la “soluzione” adottata è illusoria e conduce unicamente alla distruzione.

Conceptos “operatorios” en el fenómeno de la radicalización islamista: del sujeto al apocalipsis. A partir de entrevistas con varones que han sido encarcelados por intento de cometer o por ayudar y ser cómplices de actos de terrorismo, la pregunta central de este artículo es: ¿qué papel desempeña la radicalización islámica a nivel psíquico? Basándose en conceptos como odio, pulsión y melancolía, se analiza de qué modo están estos conectados con el hastío y el vacío –u hors-langage–. El proceso de radicalización es una manera de llenar este vacío y evitar el riesgo de colapso del self. Sin embargo, si bien la persona busca restaurar un sentido de identidad, la “solución” es un señuelo, que solo conduce a la destrucción.

responseSet.title: Summary page issue 2, 2021
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Summary page issue 2, 2021

Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique

Analytic approaches to listening have often been akin to vision, favouring the production of understanding and meaning, says Bruce Reis. His paper distinguishes a model of listening called listening-with, based on an aural approach, which instead of seeking hidden meanings, entails abiding within a space of not-knowing and of not trying to know.

Giuseppe Civitarese re-reads Bion’s brief essay “On Arrogance” from 1958, which claims that the analyst's “psychosis” finds expression in wanting to become a psychoanalyst, in aspiring to be “scientific”, and in wanting to tell the patient the truth. Anticipating Bion’s later thought, his essay forms the groundwork for a cogent criticism of the ideology of psychoanalysis, says Civitarese.

Dreams don’t simply form a defence system or an outlet of unconscious drives, says Luiz Meyer. Rather, in dreaming the psychic apparatus develops a capacity to express questions, problems and emotional experiences. Dreams also encapsulate kernels of experience into “engrams” by constantly searching for ways of addressing and readdressing emotional experience.

Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis

Johann Jung and Guillaume Gillet’s paper examines the therapeutic value of video games. Exploring the creation and use of the avatar, the paper argues that the avatar permits a game of identity with oneself that encourages access to otherness. They also look at how processes of symbolization can be mobilized in videogame worlds.

Interdisciplinary Studies

Anat Tzur Mahalel examines H. D.'s dialogue with Freud on themes of time and timelessness. In reading H. D.’s memoir Tribute to Freud (1974), the paper argues that timelessness offered H.D. a path into the realm of imagination and fantasy, not sufficiently acknowledged by Freud. The significance of timelessness is discussed in relation to André Green and Walter Benjamin.

What part does Islamist radicalization play on a psychic level? ask Annabelle Jaccard and Giorgia Tiscini. Based on interviews with men imprisoned for Islamic terrorist involvement, the paper looks at the root causes of hatred, drive and melancholy linked to boredom and emptiness, or “off language”. The radicalization process offers the illusion of filling emptiness. Yet this “solution” is a lure, leading to destruction. 217

responseSet.title: Response to “Scientific thinking styles”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to “Scientific thinking styles”

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg   nmoshe@gmail.com

Dear Editor,

Kaluzeviciute

and Willemsen’s recent article on “Scientific thinking styles” (IJP volume 101, issue 5, 2020) courageously engages the challenge of how to think about psychoanalysis as a scientific discipline. But they do not cite at least three major contributions to thinking scientifically – that is, in a manner that advances the discipline – Ricoeur’s “The Question of Proof in Freud’s Psychoanalytic Writings” (1977), the second Kuhn book, which Kuhn considered a necessary correction to his famous “paradigm” book (Kuhn 1977) The Essential Tension, and Polanyi’s Personal Knowledge (1958). Further, an article on thinking styles should reference Richards’ recent study of thinking styles and collectives in psychoanalysis (Richards 2016).

In addition, I suggest a conceptual correction: when they cite six thinking styles and add a seventh, they do not continue that these styles necessarily may be interactive, not exclusive. That is, as Freeman Dyson taught (2015), Einstein and early twentieth-century theoretical physicists may have invented the thought experiment, but, and this is a critical “but”, they did thought experiments in the expectation that empirical researchers would confirm or disprove these.

Let’s start with examples of the latter: thought experience that lead to empirical experiments. Many scientists accepted Copernicus’ heliocentric view of the solar system despite the lack of empirical proof, because it was more elegant (Dyson). It took some two hundred years for Foucault to demonstrate that Copernicus was correct. This view of the process of physics was outlined by Kuhn in his second book and by Polanyi in Personal Knowledge (1958): that there are at least eight criteria that hard scientists use to judge the validity of a theory, empirical verification being one of the eight. A further example is Einstein’s theorization of black holes, which were empirically proven only recently. Polanyi, trained in “hard sciences”, emphasizes the aesthetic aspect of judging scientific validity. Kaluzeviciute and Willemsen cite Kahnemann (and Tversky’s) emphasis on statistical proof, but do not cite the preliminary method used by these two – their voluble thought experiments/arguments heard through their office doors at the Hebrew University: statistics followed their thought experiments (Lewis 2016).

responseSet.title: Inquiries in psychoanalysis collected papers of Edna O’Shaughnessy: edited by Richard Rusbridger, Hove, Routledge, 2015, 322 pp., (pbk), ISBN: 978-1-138-79645-4, The New Library of Psychoanalysis.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inquiries in psychoanalysis collected papers of Edna O’Shaughnessy

edited by Richard Rusbridger, Hove, Routledge, 2015, 322 pp., (pbk), ISBN: 978-1-138-79645-4, The New Library of Psychoanalysis.

Hannah Solemani

Hannah Solemani

Hannah Solemani   hsolemani@aol.com

In this collection, Edna O’Shaughnessy, a leading light in both adult and child psychoanalysis, displays the full breadth of knowledge and clinical experience that has served as a guide for generations of trainees and clinicians over her career spanning 60 years. One of the first to train as a child psychotherapist at the Tavistock in the 1960s, she never lets the reader forget the presence of the infant and the child in every adult treated in analysis or psychotherapy. Following in the Kleinian tradition, O’Shaughnessy considers the work of Freud, Klein, Bion and Rosenfeld, among others. She presents the reader with her own understanding and use of difficult concepts, and develops many new ideas of her own.

This collection of papers, essays and some reviews, many originally published in the Journal of Child Psychotherapy, covers more themes than is possible to mention here, including a diverse range of subjects touching on the nature of psychic reality, subjectivity, the psychic limits of endurance and reparation, and the intricacies of defensive organizations of the personality; I mention those that are the most used and well known, although each chapter has stimulating and profound ideas. The introduction by the book’s editor Richard Rusbridger tackles both the depth and scope of O’Shaughnessy’s work in a clear and engaging style. O’Shaughnessy herself writes with great clarity and precision, using clinical material to highlight and explain complex issues, most notably her investigations into the pathological superego.

408

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The psychoanalyst’s superegos, ego ideals and blind spots: the emotional development of the clinician

by Vic Sedlak, London and New York, The New Library of Psychoanalysis, Routledge, 2019, 197 pp., £29.99 (paperback), ISBN: 978-0-367-20508-9

Denis Flynn

Denis Flynn

Denis Flynn   deniscflynn@yahoo.co.uk

In this succinct book the author sets out to show how the analyst has different superegos and ego ideals in play or in conflict within their clinical work, which may either impede understanding of the patient or sometimes guide that understanding. The author sets out contrasting accounts of the superego and neglected or largely forgotten accounts of the ego ideal, and gives his own reformulations of these theories. The case illustrations are lively and uncluttered with psychoanalytic jargon so that the interplay of unconscious internal and external interactions between patient and analyst shows through. The clinical situations described are rooted in his countertransference experience and in each case the key point is that attention to one’s countertransference is essential for the patient to be appreciated and known through painstaking transformations of the transference within the analytic process. I think it is a book of practical truth and reason – what it is like to be a psychoanalyst, both self-appraisal and criticism, and with candour the author includes what he has missed and had blind spots about. Overall it gives a perspective of psychoanalytic development within the mind of the analyst and the effects of this on his work.

At the outset the author describes how the patient “will evoke a potential aspect of the analyst’s personality” – perhaps a pathological side but not necessarily so, for the patient as well as the analyst “has an instrument that can interpret the utterances of the unconscious in other people” (2). Because of this the patient can also sense the analyst’s misunderstandings and blind spots, and this frequently comes into the dynamic of the work. He concurs with Freud that “no psychoanalyst goes further than his own complexes and his internal resistances allow” (3). (Freud 1913, SE 12: 320; 1910, SE 11: 144–145). So, in time, the patient sees the analyst as they are, unconsciously but nevertheless truly, and typically or frequently, just beyond the limits of the analyst’s own current self-understanding. The nub of the analytic work tends to coalesce around such areas – sometimes tractably, sometimes intractably.

responseSet.title: Summary Page, Issue 5, 2021
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Summary Page, Issue 5, 2021

Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique

In his reading of Winnicott's “Transitional objects and transitional phenomena,” Thomas Ogden suggests that Winnicott conceptualises the human task of creating states of being in which the individual's ideas, feelings, and bodily sensations feel alive and real. The concept of paradox captures something of the idea and experience of transitional objects and phenomena, says Ogden. He offers an example of clinical work in which an alteration in the analytic frame provokes the patient to begin to experience feelings that feel real and alive to him.

Cannibalistic fantasies underlie some core mechanisms: identification, incorporation, introjection, and internalization, says Britt-Marie Schiller; thus, cannibalism is a useful concept for understanding the dynamics of trichotillomania and trichophagy (pulling out and eating hair); both suggest a magical reconnection with what was disconnected in order to establish a bond with the mother/analyst. The passion of consuming the object of desire, symbolized by the hair (resembling an umbilical cord) captures the cannibalistic fantasy of never losing the object by destroying it.

Thomas Rabeyron offers an overview of the possible links between psychoanalytical metapsychology and contemporary work in neuroscience concerning entropy and the free energy principle. The psychic apparatus can be considered at different levels, from the most primary to the most secondary, as having the essential function of reducing entropy and free energy. Various forms of “failure” of this process of linking, regulation and transformation of energy within the psychic apparatus could be considered as the origin of different psychopathological manifestations.

Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis

In adolescent psychiatry, when patients present high-risk sexual behaviours, there is usually a traumatic sexual event in childhood, says Marion Robin, Aziz Essadek, Maurice Corcosa, and Gerard Shadilia. In the first stage the trauma is constituted and in the second adolescent stage the mnemic trace leads the traumatic experience to be reproduced within a destructured psychic apparatus that's seeking restructuration through actions. These two psychopathological stages are examined in the light of the connection between Freud's paradigms of the neurotica and the theory of fantasy.

responseSet.title: Obituary for Janine Puget
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary for Janine Puget

Julio Moreno

Julio Moreno

Julio Moreno   CONTACT Julio Moreno julmoreno@gmail.com Rep. de la India 2921, Buenos Aires, Argentina

It is not an easy thing to write an obituary so soon after the departure of a close friend, a maestra, and especially one like Janine Puget, one with such exceptional character. It is a painful task, but it also allows for the weaving together of aspects of her personal life and her prolific professional and intellectual career with those long periods of interchange and dialogue we had, which was open, sincere and sometimes sharp on both sides, hers and mine.

This text could only be the product of that spirit and that is why I dare to write it without her being here to give her opinion, as she often did on texts that I wrote, until just a few months ago. I feel lucky, however, that she was able to read and comment on my latest book, which has just been published, and I think that between the lines of what she wrote I can see a number of ideas that we discussed in the last few years.

Janine Puget was born in Marseille, France, in 1926 and died in Buenos Aires, Argentina on 4 November, 2020.

Throughout her professional life and right up to her death she was a colossal analyst: sensitive, intelligent and endowed with a distinguished and unique personality. There reigned in her life the paradox of the simultaneous presence of apparent contrasts, admirably maintained in balance between them. As an analyst, we could say both that she was classical in her outlook while also suggesting revolutionary approaches; she was sharp but charmingly pleasant, and she moved through the world in a way that was both serene and polemical; there were times when she defended the institutional to the letter, and others when she rebelled against aspects of the same. She navigated the oh-so-prevalent quicksands of Latin America, but did so with remarkable determination and this allowed her to pass through the turbulent zones of the society that she inhabited.

Her personal life was marked by traumatic events: migration in her early adolescence, the loss of members of her immediate family starting from an early age (her mother included), divorces, accidents … . on top of all of which we could add as a backdrop the turmoil of history in the world, and especially in Argentina, in the twentieth century. Right up to her death, she maintained a political position actively upheld by an impeccable ethical approach with which, it must be said, I and many others agree.

responseSet.title: Bridging cultures in Psychoanalytic work: the case of Yune
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bridging cultures in Psychoanalytic work: the case of Yune

Yune is a married endocrinologist in her early fifties. I have treated her for 7 years, the last five of which have been in psychoanalysis. Short and slender, Yune speaks quickly and is quite articulate. At the time our work began, she was married and had two daughters, aged 12 and 13. The patient was referred to me for a consultation to aid her in understanding and handling her elder daughter. The referral came from the psychiatrist who had been treating the daughter for Attention Deficit Disorder. In the first meeting, Yune told me that when her daughter didn’t study hard enough or appeared distracted, Yune herself often felt angry and would shout and throw things at her daughter. She was later filled with regret at her punitive behaviour and loss of control. In the process of discussing her parenting problems, Yune also revealed her own chronic anxiety, her habit of binging and purging that had started in college, and her current alcohol abuse, which amounted to half a bottle of wine a day. She ended up requesting her own psychotherapy with me, to which I agreed. We began at a frequency of once per week, which increased to twice weekly after a year and a half. At the point of making the decision to come more often, Yune reported a dream in which she could not find her car in a parking lot, and so couldn’t come to my office. The following week she “inadvertently” took a sleeping pill while drinking, and ended up wandering outside at night in the cold, falling asleep alone there. She was found by a security guard, who helped her return home.

After five months of twice weekly treatment I ended up recommending her coming four times per week, to which Yune agreed.

In the early consultation sessions, it became clear that Yune was ashamed of her anger and impatience with her daughter. She sincerely wanted to understand the latter’s inability to concentrate on her studies and her overall psychological state. At the same time, she was highly defensive, jumping in at the end of nearly anything I said in order to correct or contradict me. I felt pressured to respond to her incessant questions, only to have my reply discounted. Yune’s attitude seemed consistently competitive, dismissive, and condescending; at moments, this would make me feel diminished and unsure of my own perspective.

responseSet.title: Tolerating strangers in intolerant times: psychoanalytic, political and philosophical perspectives: by R. Kennedy, New York, Routledge, 2019, 156 pp., £34.99, ISBN: 9781138360259
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tolerating strangers in intolerant times: psychoanalytic, political and philosophical perspectives

by R. Kennedy, New York, Routledge, 2019, 156 pp., £34.99, ISBN: 9781138360259

Tuba Tokgoz

Tuba Tokgoz

New York

Tuba Tokgoz   tubatokgoz@gmail.com

2020 has been an unparalleled year in terms of shared political and societal trauma. The destruction brought about by the coronavirus pandemic has revealed problematic sociopolitical dynamics around the world – for example, inequalities in mortality rates depending on ethnicity and socioeconomic status. In the USA, the killings of black citizens by police have portrayed even more strongly existing racial disparities. The persistent increase of dictatorial or authoritarian leaders in many places in the world in recent years (Turkey, the USA, the UK, Brazil, Poland, the Philippines, Hungary, India, Israel, China, etc.) as well as the constant migration crises within the context of increasing global economic decline have created devastation amidst the ongoing pandemic. Kennedy’s book on tolerance is extremely relevant in the current context. Having reviewed his preceding book, Psychic Home, an internalized psychological structure that determines our sense of home and self, I felt that Kennedy continues to explore similar themes, such as identity and belonging.

416

responseSet.title: Constructing the infantile
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Constructing the infantile

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Evanston
IL
USA
Chicago
IL
USA

Bonnie Litowitz   CONTACT Bonnie E. Litowitz bonlit@gmail.com 161 E. Chicago Avenue, #46E Chicago, IL 60611, USA
Infantile body/mind/psyche, constructions in psychoanalysis, intersubjectivity

This paper addresses three dimensions that contribute to the constructions of the infantile, as they inform different psychoanalytic perspectives: the infantile body; the infantile mind; and the infantile psyche. Brief reviews of our current knowledge of the infant's physical state and earliest mention, derived from observational and experimental methodologies are presented; raising the question whether psychoanalysts should incorporate such knowledge or rely solely on clinical data. Further, the sources in Freudian texts that legitimate reconstructions of the infantile psyche are examined, while also noting theorists who have chosen alternate approaches. The paper concludes with the assertion that the dimensions which are determined as most germane to a psychoanalyst's construction of the infantile will depend ultimately upon their usefulness in establishing intersubjectivity with patients in clinical practice.

responseSet.title: Anna Freud’s loom
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anna Freud’s loom

Rye Holmboe

Rye Holmboe

London
UK

Rye Holmboe   CONTACT Rye Dag Holmboe ryedholmboe@gmail.com 3 Steeles Studios, 97a Haverstock Hill, NW3 4RN, London, UK
Anna Freud, weaving, creative process, mourning

‘Anna Freud's Loom’ examines Anna Freud's relationship to weaving. Adopting a form of weaverly thinking, I begin by offering an account of Anna Freud's work at the loom as recollected by her friend and colleague Manna Friedmann. I go on to describe how a metaphorics connected to weaving is often employed in psychoanalytic literature, especially in the writings of Sigmund Freud, which I situate in relationship to the Freud family's historical and personal relationship to textiles. The article then examines Anna Freud's use of weaving as a therapeutic tool and as a process akin to the work of mourning. For Anna Freud, weaving was, I suggest, a kind of grief-work.

Dans « Le métier à tisser d'Anna Freud » j'étudie la relation d'Anna Freud au tissage. Adoptant une forme de pensée qui emprunte au tissage, je commence par raconter le travail de tisserande d'Anna Freud d'après les souvenirs de son amie et collègue Manna Friedmann. Je poursuis ma réflexion en décrivant la façon dont la métaphore du tissage est souvent utilisée dans la littérature psychanalytique, en particulier dans les écrits de Sigmund Freud. J'associe cela à la relation historique et personnelle liant la famille de Freud au textile. J'examine ensuite l'utilisation du tissage par Anna Freud en tant qu'outil thérapeutique et processus apparenté au deuil. Je suggère l'idée que pour Anna Freud le tissage s'apparentait à un travail de deuil.

‚Anna Freuds Webstuhl‘ untersucht Anna Freuds Beziehung zum Weben. Indem ich mir ein dem Weben nachempfundenes Denken zu eigen mache, beginne ich meine Ausführungen mit einer Schilderung von Anna Freuds Arbeit am Webstuhl aus den Erinnerungen ihrer Freundin und Kollegin Manna Friedmann. Im Anschluss beschreibe ich, wie in der psychoanalytischen Literatur – insbesondere in den Schriften Sigmund Freuds – häufig eine Metaphorik des Webens verwendet wird und stelle eine Verbindung zur historischen und persönlichen Beziehung der Familie Freud zu Geweben her. Daraufhin untersucht der Beitrag Anna Freuds Nutzung des Webens als therapeutisches Werkzeug und als mit der Trauerarbeit verwandten Prozess. Ich lege nahe, dass Weben für Anna Freud eine Art Trauerwerk war.

'Il telaio di Anna Freud' prende in esame il rapporto che Anna Freud aveva con il tessere. Inizierò a sdipanare il mio pensiero raccontando del lavoro di Anna Freud al telaio sulla base dei ricordi della sua amica e collega Manna Friedmann. Descriverò poi come la letteratura psicoanalitica, e specialmente gli scritti di Sigmund Freud, facciano spesso uso di una costellazione di metafore legate al tessere: un'inclinazione che inquadrerò nel contesto dei rapporti storici e personali della famiglia Freud con i tessuti. L'articolo passa poi a esaminare l'uso che Anna Freud faceva del tessere come strumento terapeutico e come processo simile al lavoro del lutto. Come avrò modo di suggerire, per Anna Freud tessere era una specie di lavoro di lutto.

“El telar de Anna Freud” estudia la relación de Anna Freud con el tejido. El autor, entretejiendo ideas, empieza relatando el trabajo de dicha analista en el telar tal como lo recuerda su amiga y colega Manna Friedmann. Prosigue una descripción de cómo en la literatura psicoanalítica a menudo se emplean metáforas relacionadas con el tejido, sobre todo en los escritos de Sigmund Freud, lo cual es asociado con las relaciones históricas y personales de la familia Freud con los textiles. El artículo luego examina el uso que hace Anna Freud del tejido como herramienta terapéutica y como proceso parecido al de la elaboración del duelo. Se sugiere que para Anna Freud el tejido era una forma de trabajo de duelo.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Sam Semper

Sam Semper

Sam Semper   samjsemper@gmail.com

Thank you to Jill Gentile for her response to my review of her text, Feminine Law (Semper 2021). Her engagement and the opportunity to respond brings to life her argument that spaces of encounter are a vital necessity for democracy and, we might add, a psychoanalytic community built on free and respectful dialogue.

Gentile’s letter offers an overview of her larger project, which includes Feminine Law 2016 and recent elaborations, that will prove valuable for readers of her work. While I certainly agree that there is much shared in our perspectives, I respectfully disagree that my review misunderstands her argument, or misrepresents the risks of her strategy as it is rendered in Feminine Law (Gentile and Macrone 2016).

Gentile’s work on free association in psychoanalysis and free speech in the formation of US democracy is novel. Debates about the power and limits of mobilizing “the feminine” in various forms (as fugitive force in excess of the Phallic; as the renewing semiotic with the power to overturn/turn over the symbolic; as symbolic counterweight to the Phallic) have a history. The vaginal, with its complex relationship to morphology, will always be a charged topic. Gentile’s vaginal hypothesis enters the discussion at a particularly “hairy” time, to use Chu’s phrase once more – a context that does not register in the text (Chu 2019, par. 8). While never entirely absent from discourse, over the last number of years the vaginal/Vaginal has returned in psychoanalytic writing, popular culture, feminist discourse, and as a weapon in anti-transgender attacks. Gentile’s model potentially offers productive interventions on various fronts; my concern is that the limits of this strategy are not recognized, and the text itself enacts some of its dangers.

While primarily a theoretical engagement (one with a strong utopian impulse), the text also produces a gendered politics, and offers a promise of intervention and liberation for women, LGBTQ2 folks and racialized people. The clinical examples, which punctuate the text, are a place where the theory materializes. These are the moments when the gap is “figured”. It seems fair, and frankly, necessary, to read these examples and the text as a whole symptomatically.

responseSet.title: The Black Notebook. Edith Jacobson’s Prison Notes 1935/1936
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Black Notebook. Edith Jacobson’s Prison Notes 1935/1936

Judith Kessler

Judith Kessler

Berlin, Germany
jukessler@me.com

Judith Kessler  

EdithJacobson, prison, NationalSocialism, poems

The Jewish emigrant Edith Jacobson is not only known as one of the most important psychoanalysts of the 20th century, but also for her participation in the leftist resistance group Neu Beginnen and her long prison sentence for “high treason” in Berlin. She escaped with the help of Otto Fenichel and Wilhelm Reich. Personal notes from the first days of her detention were considered lost, but remained in a private household for almost 80 years. This so-called “black booklet” contains poems and essay fragments on the effects of imprisonment on female prisoners and on psychoanalytic ideas about paranoia.

The author of this paper found the prison records, made them accessible for the first time, and traces the story of this discovery. The paper sheds light on the texts it contains, the influence of detention on Jacobson's later work, and the reactions of the psychoanalytic community to her arrest.

responseSet.title: Gender and transgender: a metapsychological contribution to the genesis of the sexual ego
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender and transgender: a metapsychological contribution to the genesis of the sexual ego**

Susann Heenen-Wolff

Susann Heenen-Wolff

Belgium

Bruxelles
Belgium

Susann Heenen-Wolff   CONTACT Susann Heenen-Wolff susann.wolff@uclouvain.be Rue du Relais 105, 1050 Bruxelles
Transsexuality/transgender – gender/anatomy – construction of the ego – the “sexual” – primary/secondary identifications

Transsexuality or transgender has become an increasingly visible and widespread phenomenon in recent years. From a psychoanalytic perspective, it has so far not been possible to understand the lack of identification with one's own anatomical body. The author distinguishes between the formation of an early body ego as a result of primary identification and that of a sexual ego as part of secondary identifications. Identical and trans-identical orientation could arise against the background of how, when gender difference is discovered, the primary identification is processed, ‘translated', in an ‘après-coup’ movement.

Les phénomènes transsexuels ou transgenres sont devenus de plus en plus visibles et répandus ces dernières années. La perspective psychanalytique n'a pas permis jusqu'à présent d'éclairer la compréhension du manque d'identification de l'individu à son propre corps anatomique. L'auteure de cet article établit une distinction entre la formation d'un moi corporel précoce, issu de l'identification primaire, et celle d'un moi sexuel, produit en partie par les identifications secondaires. L'orientation identique ou trans-identique prendrait sa source dans la façon dont l'identification primaire est intégrée et « traduite » après-coup, au moment de la découverte de la différence des sexes.

Transsexualität bzw. Transidentität ist in den letzten Jahren ein zunehmend sichtbares und sich verbreitendes Phänomen geworden. Aus psychoanalytischer Perspektive ist bisher nicht gelungen, das Ausbleiben von Identifizierung mit dem eigenen anatomischen Körper nachzuvollziehen. Die Autorin unterscheidet zwischen der Ausbildung eines frühen Körper-Ichs in Folge primärer Identifizierung und der eines geschlechtlichen Ichs im Rahmen sekundärer Identifizierungen. Idente und transidente Orientierung könnte entstehen vor dem Hintergrund, wie mit dem Entdecken des Geschlechtsunterschiedes die primäre Identifizierung in einer nachträglichen Bewegung verarbeitet, „übersetzt“ wird.

Negli ultimi anni i fenomeni della transessualità e dell'identità transgenere sono divenuti sempre più visibili e diffusi. Finora non è stato possibile comprendere la mancanza di identificazione con il proprio corpo anatomico da una prospettiva psicoanalitica. L'autrice distingue tra la formazione di un io corporeo precoce, frutto dell'identificazione promaria, e quella di un io sessuale che farebbe invece parte del quadro delle identificazioni secondarie. Alla base dell'orientamento identico o trans-identico potrebbero esserci una serie di fattori: non solo il modo e il momento in cui è stata scoperta la differenza sessuale, ma anche il modo in cui l'identificazione primaria viene elaborata e 'tradotta' in un movimento di 'après-coup''.

En los últimos años, la transexualidad o transgénero se ha convertido en un fenómeno cada vez más visible y extendido. Aún no ha sido posible comprender, desde una perspectiva psicoanalítica, la falta de identificación con el propio cuerpo anatómico. La autora distingue entre la formación de un yo corporal temprano como resultado de la identificación primaria y la formación de un yo sexual en el marco de las identificaciones secundarias. La orientación idéntica y transidéntica podría surgir en la manera en que se procesa la identificación primaria en el contexto del descubrimiento de la diferencia entre los sexos, “traducida” en un movimiento après-coup.

responseSet.title: Alongside: Regarding the otherness of Warren Poland
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alongside: Regarding the otherness of Warren Poland

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

Cambridge, MA
USA

Alfred Margulies   alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

“Man has no harbor, time has no shore./ It flows, and we pass on.” Lamartine (personal correspondence from Jacob Arlow to Poland; quoted in Poland discussion in Margulies 2014)

Reviewer, interrupted

Pre-pandemic, embarking on a review of two extensive volumes of collected papers of an esteemed colleague, I wrote these notes, which I have edited for clarity:

Impression: reviewing his first book, the reader encounters Poland in his historical moment. Clinical theory is shifting and, inadequate to the task, theoretical language is under revision, evolving. Poland’s writing performs the psychoanalytic journey itself and captures his distinctive way of approaching another—an Other—creating a conversation that continually opens up with ceaseless, restless inquiry. Poland has a deep appreciation of what Loewald (1960, 1972) called “the something-more” of life, the becoming. An informed reader knows where things are heading, and so my interest is observing how Poland engages the issues of his moment, with all of the uncertainty and fog of change. And, years later in my now knowing how the field will evolve, I’m looking for what’s unique and enduring in Poland’s approach once one subtracts the shifts in theoretical fashion. What does endure, it seems to me, is Poland’s distinctive psychoanalytic way-of-being. I say way-of-being because his approach is so much more than theory, or experience, or knowledge: it is how he comports oneself, how one lives in a psychoanalytic way … 

There is then Poland’s aspirational, psychoanalytic way-of-being (and just what is that?), but there is also, inextricably, the unique person of Warren Poland.

Precisely because “uniqueness of the other” is so much of what Poland’s work is about, to leave out his uniqueness would leave a flattened-out review. I hear Poland’s voice in his writing; his presence comes through, how he greets me—and others if you are standing nearby—with a big smile, a ready joke, drawing into conversation, quickly, almost effortlessly, generously—he has a way with people. Joie de vivre, the real thing—you feel it reverberate, the shared smile, the personal connection. His wise presence stands next to his “unflinching candor” about life and pain, the two frame one another, figure ground.

[[RunningHead]]

Experiencing the Body: A Psychoanalytic Dialogue on Psychosomatics, by Jacques Press, Fotis Bobos, Jörg Frommer, Marina Perris-Myttas, Eva Schmid-Gloor, Bérengère de Senarclens, Christian Seulin, Luigi Solano, Nick Temple, Catherine Humble, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 158 pp., £26.39, ISBN: 9780367181574

Julian Stern

Julian Stern

London, UK
Julianstern@hotmail.com
JStern@tavi-port.nhs.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3415-5022

Julian Stern  

This slim and dense volume is the result of a long-standing dialogue and collaboration between psychoanalysts working in at least six European countries who have worked together over a sustained period, under the auspices of the European Psychoanalytical Federation since 2013, focusing specifically on the field of psychosomatics - its practice, its theory and its debates.

The task and methodology are described by the senior author Jacques Press (from Switzerland):

It was time to tackle the various theoretical approaches in the field of psychosomatics. We were intent on rooting our reflections in clinical work. … Exposing ourselves through our daily clinical practice not only contributed to creating friendly ties in the group and fostering dynamics of exchange and interaction that were respectful of our differences, it also contributed to the formation of a group identity … we each had to confront unfamiliar ways of thinking and tolerate differences between us, but we also had to confront and make explicit what was often implicit in our own way of thinking and thereby become more aware of it. (1)

As a result, the book is designed in an unusual way. After two cases are presented in the opening section, a further nine chapters ensue, each of them a triad of contributions on an important theme, always referring back to the two initial clinical examples and to the theoretical models that inform each writer’s work. Each triad consists of two authors offering their perspective of the topic under discussion, and then a third contributor, a commentator, “an avatar of the ‘silent observer in our workshops’ discusses the two viewpoints,” at times challenging them, and striving to account for their visible and less visible divergences and convergences. “Bringing a third eye to bear on things, the commentator opens up a new space, putting the two authors’ contributions in perspective and shedding a new light on them” (Press, 4).

Right at the end of the book, after the two clinical examples and the nine tripartite chapters, Jacques Press writes a short concluding chapter, posing new questions, bringing some of the debates together and celebrating the diversity of views, the divergences and the convergences.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Interventions in Pregnancy and Infancy, Clinical and Theoretical Perspectives, by Bjorn Salomonsson, London and New York, Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group, 2018, 296 pp., £32 (paperback), ISBN: 978-0-8153-5905-0

Shirley B. Hiscock

Shirley B. Hiscock

Shirley.B.Hiscock@icloud.com

Shirley B. Hiscock  

Bjorn Salomonsson’s book follows from his earlier research and publications on psychoanalytically inspired therapies with parents and infants, on child analysis and on case presentation methods. It is a description of his work at the Child Health Centre (CHC) using a psychoanalytically informed model that combines supervision, therapeutic consultations and brief interventions. He differentiates the CHC work from mainstream mental health centres in Sweden and prefaces the presentation of his method as informed by his own clinical experience, which does not include for instance LGBTQ families and families with adopted children. He understands that the work model he uses is not restricted to Scandinavian countries, that it can be applied broadly.

The importance of the analyst’s capacity for containment, open-mindedness and holding the internal frame, “reading between the lines”, is at the core of the work. His consultation model, its usefulness described in relation to interventions offered in the early stages of pregnancy and delivery, is based on Meltzer’s ideas of the need for the analyst to “preside over the setting”, the experienced therapist’s robust professional identity guiding the work and choice of technique. A fully fledged transference with the analyst is neither possible nor desirable in such consultations, although it is nevertheless present. The transference can facilitate communication and must be kept in mind by the analyst in their attempts to understand the mother’s personality. The consultation work, also described as brief therapy, includes assessing and communicating with the baby, and Salomonsson’s perspective on it is presented in great detail. It is clear the importance of clinical experience and a sound analytic stance to deliver the work he describes.

The institutional frame in the CHC, the interface with its users and the supervision work with nurses are closely described and examined. He comments for instance on the importance of both staff and service users not turning a blind eye to emotional problems. The parent’s shame can conceal emotional turmoil and this can end up repeating between nurses and doctors who may also ignore important signs of emotional distress. Supervision is understood as an important tool to address resistance to naming and addressing emotional difficulties. The supervision model focuses on emotional reactions in nurses and their patients in order to help nurses to identify signs of emotional difficulties and attend to them as appropriate. The description of the supervision work evokes thoughts on the complexities of the role of being a professional in the institution while also supervising fellow staff of the same institution, and how the institutional dynamics might present themselves in such work.

responseSet.title: Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): The Infantile and its Dimension in the Finding of Unconscious Fantasy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): The Infantile and its Dimension in the Finding of Unconscious Fantasy

Matthew Johnson

Matthew Johnson

Matthew Johnson  

Chair: Stefano Bolognini

Panelists: Paula Ellman, Nancy Goodman, Douglas Chavis

Discussants: Dana Birksted-Breen, Abel Fainstein

Stefano Bolognini began the panel by stating how all of the panel papers were diverse, emotionally moving and not only consisted of deep reflections on psychoanalytic theory and technique, but also considered the cultural influence psychoanalysis has had on society and humanity in general.

The first paper, entitled “Finding the Infantile, Finding Unconscious Fantasy with the Traumas of Now and Then”, by Nancy Goodman, introduced Goodman’s (2015) model of “finding theater” where there are “multiple stages jutting out into space of the theater” with ladders that connect the stages, and separate stages that produce sound and music out to the audience. These distinct stages, according to Goodman, are in place to transport the baby’s internal world to blend with the “feel, body, sensation, and imagery” of the developing unconscious fantasy life, and there is a “special theater” where the mind is open to both analyst and analysand, joined through transference and countertransference in both wishes and fears. According to Goodman, the way that the “too much” of trauma becomes symbolized assists our acceptance of how the unconscious infantile also becomes symbolized, and that it is the unconscious reverberation in the minds of both analyst and analysand that allows the darkened, repressed, yet unsymbolized stages of the repressed to come to the forefront.

The space of “finding the theater,” according to Goodman, is “dynamic and stable,” and can be used as a structure for analytic work to hold and contain the disarray of both life and death forces. Goodman asserts that despite the changes necessary to continue analytic work in the time of COVID-19, the model of finding theatre has surprisingly continued to function as a valuable concept, and fantasy has often been absorbed in the absolute terror experienced by both the analyst and analysand throughout the crisis. Further, she suggests that it was perhaps “analytic listening” itself that has invited the entire mind to become known despite major changes within the traditional frame. Goodman goes onto discuss how two crises, the previously mentioned COVID-19, as well as the murder of George Floyd in the USA, have awakened the infantile, especially as it pertains to annihilation fears; and thus the “witnessing function” of analyst and patient has been helpful to “open space, open the curtains, and identify scenes with their unconscious wishes and fears and affects”.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): COWAP Panel, Infantile Regression in Men and Women in Times of Flight, Trauma and Displacement

Marion Schoenfeld

Marion Schoenfeld

Marion Schoenfeld  

Chair: Paula L. Ellman

Presenter: Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber

Discussants: Patricia Alkolombre, Jhuma Basak, Cecile Bassen, Cristina Saottini

Paula Ellman (USA) introduced the panel theme - the way in which regression to the infantile appears in refugees and their helpers.

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber (Germany) has been highly active in working with refugees in Germany since the world refugee crisis became acute in 2015. She briefly gave an overview of the interdisciplinary approach developed in a model refugee community/village established for particularly vulnerable groups, including women and children. The pilot project was called “STEP-BY-STEP”. “FIRST STEPS” were offered in the reception centre, the “Michaelis-Village” (see, for example, Leuzinger-Bohleber et al. 2016).

The project provided the refugees with a reliable daily structure and interaction with empathic helpers. These alternative relational experiences sought to strengthen resilience and avoid further traumatization in the reception centre. Meaningful activities were encouraged to restore a sense of agency and human dignity. The centre provided short-term psychotherapeutic work, including crisis intervention, art therapy for children, and therapeutic groups. These “FIRST STEPS” aimed to support the refugees psychoanalytically, help them to move out into the wider community and get on with their lives. This would make possible more intensive work. Those refugees who later felt that they required or wanted more in-depth therapy were able to return for psychoanalytic sessions (“SECOND STEPS”).

Marianne then presented two short case studies to illustrate that it is possible to do in-depth psychoanalytic work with refugees, but often only in the sense of a “SECOND STEP”. The support provided by the “FIRST STEPS” was essential, as was a latency period when the refugees established themselves in the external reality of their new home in Germany. After such a latency period, it is often the reactivation of unresolved unconscious conflicts, which are linked to embodied memories of trauma, torture, persecution and flight, that motivates refugees to seek longer term psychoanalytic treatment.

[[RunningHead]]

Obituary: Prof Jorge Canestri

Dana Birksted-Breen , Jacqueline Amati-Mehler and Heribert Blass

Heribert Blass

Heribert Blass  

Jorge Canestri came from a family who led long lives. He liked to talk about this. He had so much more to give, but sadly he never reached the age of 80. Jorge died on 7 May 2021, leaving behind his wife, daughter, two sons and four grandchildren. Jorge’s death was a painful loss for his family and friends, the Italian Psychoanalytical Association, his colleagues at the International Journal and the worldwide psychoanalytical community.

Jorge Canestri was born in Buenos Aires on 19 August 1942. Early in his life, his father introduced him to the importance of language and literature: the “sayings” of poets are necessary for the young soul, his father told him. And so the young Jorge was sent to attend lectures at the Academia Argentina de Letras, where, in Jorge’s later words, he felt at times surprised, amazed and bored. It was at these meetings that he met Jorge Luis Borges, and without understanding the content of his works, Jorge recalled the sense of magic in Borges’s words. The two Jorges would meet again in Rome in 1984, when Borges agreed to an interview with Jorge and they talked about the poetic discovery of childhood by Charles Dickens and Freud. The study of the magic of language remained a lifelong passion for Jorge, as did his love of fine art. As a teenager and a young man, Jorge painted pictures before he turned to medicine. These cultured roots in his youth informed Jorge’s later wide-ranging interests. His preference for the close, contemplative absorption of painting and literature, and his passion for the humanities, along with science and mathematics, remained with Jorge throughout his life.

In 1966, Jorge studied medicine and psychiatry, graduating with First Class Honours from the Universidad Nacional de Buenos Aires. From 1966 to 1972 he completed his psychiatric and psychotherapeutic residency in Buenos Aires under the training analysts Pichon Rivière and Mauricio Goldenberg. At the same time, he did his psychoanalytic training at the Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina (APA) with Madeleine Baranger, and in 1973, aged 31, he became a full Member of the IPA.

responseSet.title: Introduction to the case of Yune
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to the case of Yune

It is a great pleasure for me to introduce this time the Analyst at Work section of the IJP. Once more, it delivers a presentation of detailed clinical material on the analysis of an adult patient, this time round carried out by a Korean psychoanalyst and discussed by renowned colleagues from Switzerland and Australia.

The case is very well presented and offers ample background information which turns out to be of some importance for the reader considering the context of the case. This last fact should be taken into account given that it goes to the heart of this clinical debate: the reader will find here clinical material from analysis which takes place in Korea, but is commented on by two analysts who come from the European tradition of psychoanalytic training.

We must here emphasise how much is added to the clinical debate by having the possibility to take into account the context in which each psychoanalytical process takes place. The culture, the history, the interplay between these two last factors, and the singularity of the meeting between analyst and patient is something that comes through when we read the three texts.

These days, it is simply not possible to understand an analysis centring only in what happens to a patient or an analyst, nor solely in the relationship between transference and countertransference at the heart of the analytic dyad, as was suggested by the Barangers with their concept of the analytic field.

Psychoanalysis has developed in recent years, taking on board the idea that it is imperative to take the context into account and that this, changing as it does from one era to another and from place to place, has an important impact on the very practice of psychoanalysis.

But there is another detail which makes this presentation special: we are looking here at material submitted by an analyst who is exercising their practice in a region which we could consider younger in relation to the development of psychoanalysis, and who also forms part of a group of analysts which is perhaps less well-known and which is indeed developing as I write.

responseSet.title: Does the repetition compulsion really have a purpose?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Does the repetition compulsion really have a purpose?

Michel Sanchez-Cardenas

Michel Sanchez-Cardenas

Nantes
France

Michel Sanchez-Cardenas   CONTACT Michel Sanchez-Cardenas drmsanchezcardenas@wanadoo.fr 29 bis rue de la Chézine, Nantes 44100, France

The International Journal of Psychoanalysis (IJP; 2020, 101 (6):1162–1214) devoted a section to the repetition compulsion. It provided an overview of the hypotheses that have been formulated as to its genesis. After a brief review of these, I would, however, like to introduce an additional hypothesis. It seems to me that, in addition to those presented, it could be argued that the repetition compulsion has no other purpose than … repetition itself. Can we even talk, strictly speaking, about a “purpose” or is repetition more just an automatism or some kind of self-replicating programme?

A brief reminder

Here, very schematically, are some of the hypotheses mentioned in the IJP: In “Remembering, Repeating and Working-Through” (1914) Freud indicated that repetition could testify to a repressed meaning remaining active until remembered. The analytic treatment, allowing this remembering, in particular by the working-through in the transference, was to enable the lifting of this repression and, consequently, the end of the repetition. In “Beyond the Pleasure Principle” (1920), he indicated other possible meanings of pathological repetitions. These could be seen as bringing its pleasure quotient to a severely punitive superego. But it could also respond to disturbances to the psyche’s protective shield by excessive (and therefore traumatic) quantities. By reversing activity and passivity and by enabling identification with the aggressor, it offered the prospect of mastery.

Weiss, not without emphasizing that repetition could have different sources and could thus be “a river with different tributary streams” (Weiss 2020, 1172–1187), insisted that repetition could, particularly from a Kleinian perspective, arise from the impossibility of accessing a sufficiently symbolized unconscious reparation process. Access to which, especially when dreams finally arrive during treatment, can make it possible to shed the repetition.

Blass recalls that “Beyond the Pleasure Principle”, although linking repetition to the death drive, also offers passages where Freud links it to Eros and to its non-narcissistic object relations (Blass 2020, 1188–1207). Thus, referring like Weiss to Riviere, she comes to show how the impossibility of building a reparation process vis-à-vis the internal object leads to a preference for self-sacrifice (in order to preserve the pain related to repetition, particularly via negative therapeutic reactions) instead of tolerating a destructive unconscious aggressiveness vis-à-vis the object.

responseSet.title: Experiences in Groups as a Key to “Late” Bion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Experiences in Groups as a Key to “Late” Bion

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Pavia, Italy
gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

Group, field theory, Bion, O, basic assumption, reversive perspective

In order to understand the ‘late’ Bion, i.e. his production from Learning from Experience onwards, the most difficult and controversial, it is worth re-reading not only the essays in Second Thoughts, but also Experiences in Groups. The hypothesis of this work is that Bion spent his life as a scholar - probably without even being fully aware of it, since he never talks about it - to transpose his theory of groups into his theory of individual psycho-analysis. To illustrate this thesis, the author systematically compares some of the principles of both. Going back to Experiences in Groups, then, is also useful for another reason. Bion revolutionises theory but still works as a Kleinian. Despite brilliant suggestions, he does not fully develop a new technique. To have a tool box that anyone can use, we have to wait the analytic field theory, which is an original development of his thought. In this model the analyst sees in the analytical pair not two isolated subjects that interact, but a group. There is no ‘fact’ of analysis that cannot be heard as unconsciously co-created. Here then, highlighting the group inspiration of late Bion's helps us to grasp the meaning of this technical principle, so easily misunderstood, and vice versa.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen   Dana.Birksted-breen@dib.me.uk

 … Have you not done tormenting me with your accursed time! It's abominable! When! When! One day, is that not enough for you.

(Waiting for Godot, Samuel Beckett)

The experience of time has been disorganized by the pandemic, too fast and too slow, or even ‘out of time’, waiting time. The uncanny experience of the world situation, an unleashing of the furies.

I chose Penelope from the myth of Ulysses for the cover of the Journal this year because temporality is not only always at the heart of psychoanalysis (Birksted-Breen, 2016) – at the juncture and disjuncture between conscious and unconscious – but takes on new experiential forms in the current situation.

Referring to Penelope’s weaving, Laplanche mentions Homer’s use of the Greek word for ‘undoing’, which is the same word as ‘analysing’, and he points out that unweaving is necessary in order to create a new fabric (Laplanche, 1999). It is part of the work of mourning. Unpicking the tapestry in order to omnipotently stop time (Birksted-Breen, 2003), or unweaving in order to create something new, depending on the capacity to mourn.

The new experience of enforced remote sessions during the pandemic has enabled the possibility of ‘unpicking’ the threads of the components of our usual setting, enabling authors to develop ideas about what is normally left silent. It seems creativity isn’t lacking in these times of enforced restrictions, giving rise to papers concerned with the new situation. In this issue, Jonathan Lear rereads Freud’s paper ‘On Transience’, and shows how it might be read differently in the time of the pandemic with the thought of a world-catastrophe in mind. Rosine Perelberg looks at how telephone analysis can lead to important analytic developments.

In this issue, we are also very pleased to announce the winners of the IJP Prize for the best paper related to the coronavirus crisis. The prize was announced last May and we received a large number of interesting submissions. In the end, two papers shared the prize and they are published in this issue: ‘Covid 19 in five acts’, by Piotr Krzakowski, which discusses the unfolding of the pandemic in France and the questions it raised and illuminated; ‘Playing and virtual reality’, co-authored by Monica Bomba from Italy and Johanna Velt and Julia-Flore Alibert from France, on carrying out psychoanalysis remotely with children.

responseSet.title: Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): Mechanisms of Change. A View from Clinical Observation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): Mechanisms of Change. A View from Clinical Observation

Marina de Litvan

Marina de Litvan

Marina de Litvan  

Chair: Robert White

Presenters: Siri Erika Gullestad, Luisa Pérez Suquilvide, Robert White

Discussant: Margaret Ann Fitzpatrick Hanly

The IPA Clinical Observation Committee has pioneered a method to track transformations in patients along analysis, called the Three-Level Model (3-LM). In a group of peers, a psychoanalyst presents clinical hours from the beginning of an analysis, a middle section, and a later section, typically over 4-6 years of work. The group develops themes from the beginning sections, called anchor points, and follows those themes in the later material, observing the presence of negative change, no change and positive change. The work of the group is always bottom-up, starting with the phenomenological description and avoiding school terminology. In level two, the themes are sorted into categories of psychological functioning, allowing the group to understand in what functional areas there has been change or not.

After 10 years of work with this model, for this panel the focus chosen was level three, “Explanatory hypotheses of change”. Looking at the analyst’s interventions and the patients’ responses, the aim was, through microanalysis of the therapeutic dialogue, to explore mechanisms of change - although, as Siri Gullestad addresses, one cannot abstract mechanisms of change by looking solely at the analyst’s interventions.

The work remains bottom-up, taking as a unit a theme of the patient, how the analyst intervened, and how the patient then heard the intervention. Examples of questions are: What is the intent of the analyst? To what psychic space was the intervention addressed? To what level of consciousness? To wish or defence? Conflict or deficit? And finally, how was the intervention heard by the patient and did it produce change? Not all these questions were addressed in this panel.

In this panel three different clinical materials were presented. Ana, David and Kim’s cases belong to three main regions of the IPA: Latin America, North America and Europe. The analyst presenting on the panel was different from the original analyst of the case. Due to time, the presentations were focused on central aspects of the analysis and important mechanisms of change observed through study of the interaction/dialogue. The Chair of the Clinical Observation Committee, Margaret Ann Fitzpatrick Hanly, was the Discussant for the panel.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The psychoanalytic ear and the sociological eye: toward an American independent tradition

by Nancy J. Chodorow, London & New York, Routledge, 2020, 290 pp., £27.99, ISBN: 978-0367134235

Paula Ellman

Paula Ellman

Rockville
MD
USA

Paula Ellman   Paula.ellman@gmail.com

Chodorow offers us a retrospective on her contributions to the American Independent tradition, yet not in terms of her ideas on gender and femininity. As her life has bridged two disciplines, psychoanalysis and sociology, these contributions focus on an area of psychoanalysis that is also an intermingling, on the place in between. Chodorow's impressive effort demonstrates her capacity to write in such a way as to integrate, to build bridges, rather than to polarize and divide. Over the course of her career Chodorow presented at a multitude of plenaries and lectures. In this book she assembles her past writings along with newer thinking. Most have been published at an earlier time.

Her preface is a discussion of her career, moving between sociology and psychoanalysis, and her interest in bringing individuality into sociology, and culture and society into psychoanalysis. She identifies a sociological trend in American psychoanalysis in the form of an “Independent American tradition”. Similar to the “Middle School” British Independent traditions synthesizing Klein and Anna Freud, Chodorow describes the American Independents as synthesizing ego psychology and interpersonal psychoanalysis, and calls this “intersubjective ego psychology”, exemplified by Loewald and Erikson. It is clear that there have been specific individuals to whom Chodorow has felt close who have influenced her thinking in this area of “intersubjective ego psychology”, especially Poland and McLaughlin, and she offers us her perspective on these influential contributors.

Her first chapter, “The American Independent tradition” is the core of this book's theme, a historic-cultural study, as Chodorow brings her sociological lens to the development of the North American psychoanalytic tradition. Chodorow further defines the middle place, “the intersubjective ego psychologists”, the terrain between classical structural and contemporary ego psychology, and classical interpersonal and contemporary relational psychoanalysis.
