responseSet.title: Race in psychoanalysis: aboriginal populations in the mind: by Celia Brickman, New York, Routledge, 2018, 234 pp., £25.89, ISBN: 9781138749399
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Race in psychoanalysis: aboriginal populations in the mind

by Celia Brickman, New York, Routledge, 2018, 234 pp., £25.89, ISBN: 9781138749399

Romy Reading

Romy Reading

New York
NY

Romy Reading   romy@romyreading.com

In this revised edition of the book, originally published in 2003 as Aboriginal Populations in the Mind: Race and Primitivity in Psychoanalysis, Ms Brickman, psychoanalyst and scholar-in-residence at the Center for Religion and Psychotherapy of Chicago, offers an illuminating and innovative deconstruction of the racial and colonial assumptions embedded in psychoanalytic theory. Equipped with a mastery of post-colonial theory, critical race theory, feminist critique and theories from religious studies, as well as a sophisticated understanding of psychoanalytic theory, she offers us a radical perspective on Freud's meta-psychological, cultural and clinical thought. Ms Brickman offers cogent summaries of Freud's writings and extrapolates numerous examples from a vast body of clinical and cultural texts demonstrating a deep familiarity with his oeuvre. Unlike many Freudian critiques, Ms Brickman deftly balances her call for reconstruction with a valorizing of the revolutionary and emancipatory aspects of Freud's thinking. In this book, she takes a meta-psychoanalytic approach and turns the psychoanalytic method toward psychoanalytic theory itself. In doing so, she guides us toward the creation of a reparative psychoanalytic perspective and approach that ultimately aims for greater inclusivity and liberation.

Ms. Brickman begins by charting a rigorously researched genealogy of the colonial and racist discourses upon which early anthropological and evolutionary theories were constructed, the very ideas that Freud utilized in developing his theories of the unconscious, psychic and libidinal development, and human civilization. In tracing the origins of the racialized subtext of psychoanalytic theory, she focuses on the concept of primitivity. She sees primitivity as a racially problematic construct that functions as the lynchpin of many of Freud's theories. She explicates this thesis through the use of numerous Freudian texts, placing considerable attention on Group Psychology and The Analysis of the Ego, Totem and Taboo, Moses and Monotheism, Papers on Technique, and Civilization and Its Discontents. Employing interdisciplinary thinking and a careful analysis of these texts, she builds an impressively coherent argument given the breadth of her sources. She articulates how primitivity encompasses 642

responseSet.title: The Environmental Crisis on the Couch: edited by Luc Magnenat, Foreword by Dominique Bourg, Paris, Editions In Press, 2019, 272 pp., €20, ISBN:9782848355153
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Environmental Crisis on the Couch

edited by Luc Magnenat, Foreword by Dominique Bourg, Paris, Editions In Press, 2019, 272 pp., €20, ISBN:9782848355153

Laurence Bittar-Fulpius

Laurence Bittar-Fulpius

Switzerland

Laurence Bittar-Fulpius   lbittar@hin.ch

This book of burning topicality is based on four foundational texts, written by scientists, writers and psychoanalysts, all attuned to the environmental crisis. This inner ring of authors is then debated by an outer ring: scientists, philosophers and psychoanalysts whose attunement to the environmental crisis resonates with each other. The readers themselves could form a further encompassing ring of resonance, nurtured by emotions and thoughts aroused by the reading of this book, and driven to action by their engagement.

The texts are introduced by Dominique Bourg, philosopher and assistant professor of geosciences and environment, and Luc Magnenat, psychoanalyst. A reflection is initiated about the neoliberal society in which we live – with its violence, its injustices and its risk of collapse – and its resonance to the period before World War II, about which Freud wrote in his text Civilization and its Discontents. For several decades, humanity has been confronted with alarming scientific data, including population data, and the state of resources and of planet Earth, without any individual or collective human conduct having being able to fully take on the situation. Over the course of the Industrial Revolution, and then the digital, robotics and genetic revolutions, humanity has acquired certain powers, which have modified and put the environment in crisis, even though humanity is only a “link in the chain of the ecosystem of life” and depends on the biosphere for its own life. This environmental crisis has in return affected humanity by bringing it to a state of crisis and preventing it from reflecting. Among the many disciplines that are interested in ecological issues, past psychoanalytic silences are addressed. Resistances to the environmental crisis are compared to similar resistances, perhaps not so distant, that are observed in the face of psychoanalysis; both speak of deep anxieties and of things we do not wish to acknowledge. And yet the search for self-truth, curiosity, tolerance of ignorance and doubt, the deployment of rich and free thought, which are all part of psychoanalytic work, could constitute valuable contributions to the environmental crisis, and it is these that constitute the fabric of this book.

responseSet.title: Discussion of “Bridging cultures in Psychoanalytic work”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of “Bridging cultures in Psychoanalytic work”

Louise Gyler

Louise Gyler

Louise Gyler   CONTACT Louise Gyler louise.gyler@bigpond.com

Dr Rho introduces us to his patient, Yune, a physician in her early fifties and gives an account of his work with her that takes place over seven years. Interestingly, and like many referrals for treatment in countries where psychoanalytic thinking is beginning to take root, the referral is not for psychoanalysis, however, we regard it, nor is it even conceived as directly for Yune herself, but rather as a consultation to help her in understanding and parenting her daughter who suffers from so-diagnosed Attention Deficit Disorder. Following the assessment with Dr Rho, Yune requests psychotherapy for herself. I think what Dr Rho glosses here is the sensitive understanding and tact involved in initiating a move from the interpersonal parent-child focussed referral to one where the focus is on the individual’s inner world. What was it that Dr Rho was able to convey to his narcissistically vulnerable patient with impulse, identity and substance issues, which interested her in exploring her own mind and functioning?

As reported, Yune, an academically gifted child whose parents needed her to excel, appears to have suffered anxiety and loneliness since childhood. Her parents are reported as misunderstanding the role of play, enjoyment and imagination in childhood, seeming to (mis)believe that it detracts from learning and development rather than supporting it. Yune’s parents are described as using anger, physical punishment and shaming to render Yune compliant to their aspirations for obedience, discipline and constrained behaviour. I am inclined to think that not only psychological but also cultural factors influenced by Confucian values about filial piety and obedience are involved in shaping the parental attitudes to child rearing. In childhood, Yune reported that she held a phantasy that she would be rescued by a ‘hero’ – this phantasy may have contributed to enabling her to begin work with Dr Rho by generating a positive transference and hope in a psychoanalytic process and balancing the more evident demeaning and competitive stance towards the analyst. Yune reported that she developed an eating disorder and substance misuse in college. Yune appears to attempt to manage her narcissistic vulnerabilities through splitting and projection: the internal object is split into inferior/superior, dominant/submissive, and idealised/devalued with one side projected. In the early years of her work with Dr Rho, the dynamics of this splitting are manifested and communicated in her analysis with Dr Rho often in projective identification with the negative side of the split.

responseSet.title: A case for Lear
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A case for Lear

Jason Vega

Jason Vega

New York
NY
USA

Jason Vega   jasonwheelerphd@gmail.com

Introduction

Wisdom Won from Illness (2017) is Jonathan Lear’s second book of essays, 15 papers written between 2002 and 2015. The title comes from a 1936 lecture by Thomas Mann written for Freud’s 80th birthday (Lear 1990, xiii). Lear has used the phrase before: “Freud … began from pathology, from the failure of the world or the human to be good enough. Psychoanalysis, in that sense, is a wisdom won from illness” (1990, 157). Later he suggests: “Plato’s psychology, like Freud’s, is ‘wisdom won from illness’” (1998, 238). It seems this phrase has grown on Lear over the years and finally earned a titular place.

Although the essays in Wisdom (2017), as in his first collection (Lear 1998), are excellent value for people interested in getting to know Lear’s ideas, they also present to the reader a significant demand for work. They span a very wide range, beginning with a Platonic-Nietzschean view of the relationships between sickness, health and humanity in the title essay, and including integrations of Aristotle and Freud, Lear’s distinctive concept of irony, Plato’s psychology, a study in comparative technique and termination, concepts of love and the soul that Lear has been working out for decades, elaborations of Loewald, and an 379

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and anxiety: from knowing to being: by Chris Mawson, New York and London, Routledge, 2019, 217 pp., £34.99 (paperback), ISBN 9780367152277
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and anxiety: from knowing to being

by Chris Mawson, New York and London, Routledge, 2019, 217 pp., £34.99 (paperback), ISBN 9780367152277

Richard Zimmer

Richard Zimmer

Great Barrington
Mass.
USA

Richard Zimmer   richardbzimmermd@gmail.com

Clinical psychoanalysis, at its core, concerns itself with the understanding and interpretation of anxiety and the internal conflicts that underlie it. In this book, Chris Mawson takes a closer look at the anxiety that analysts encounter in clinical practice, the components of that anxiety and the way those components are perceived and communicated about. In particular, Mawson concerns himself with what he calls ontological anxiety, anxiety less born in object relational conflict than in fears of non-being, of loss or disintegration of the self. The world of internal objects and the conflicts and phantasies concerning these objects, for Mawson, proceeds from the need to manage these more primordial anxieties about being and non-being; and these two realms of anxiety are perceived, and clinically addressed through different but complementary ways of listening to, and being with, the patient. As a theoretician, Mawson is deeply steeped in the Kleinian-Bionian tradition, and his formulations draw particularly on Bion's ideas about transformations in “K” and “O”. But he draws as well on philosophy and dramaturgy; and his ideas about anxiety and about communication in the analytic situation are embedded in his understanding of these two fields.

403

responseSet.title: Finding unconscious fantasy in narrative, trauma and body pain: a clinical guide: edited by Paula L. Ellman and Nancy R. Goodman, London and New York, Routledge, 2017, 226 pp., $52.95, ISBN: 978-1-138-955001-1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Finding unconscious fantasy in narrative, trauma and body pain: a clinical guide

edited by Paula L. Ellman and Nancy R. Goodman, London and New York, Routledge, 2017, 226 pp., $52.95, ISBN: 978-1-138-955001-1

Carla Rentrop

Carla Rentrop

Carla Rentrop   carlarentrop@gmail.com

What is unconscious fantasy? Where and how can it be found? The analytic process of “finding”, presented by Paula Ellman and Nancy Goodman in their book Finding Unconscious Fantasy in Narrative, Trauma and Body Pain: A Clinical Guide, actualizes this concept and locates it in the clinical arena.

The role of unconscious fantasy with regard to symptom development in trauma, and the recognition of its derivatives for therapeutic action, integrates and unifies the field of psychoanalysis. Analytic cases of severely traumatized patients who express and experience affects and psychic material concretely and somatically illustrate how the finding process can “transform pain into psychic suffering and bring the unsymbolized into a symbolizing process” (Ellman 2017b, 41).

An international forum of renowned writers and practitioners from diverse theoretical backgrounds, many of whom have written extensively on trauma and somatic symptoms, examine unconscious fantasy in their own clinical work. Eight cases, four of which are “observed” by two outside analysts from differing theoretical perspectives, elucidate the process and its therapeutic action. Using basic psychoanalytic foundations these process-filled case examples reveal how understanding and use of unconscious fantasy in transference/countertransference manifestations, dreams, free associations and bodily experience become transformative moments in treatment.

411

responseSet.title: The therapeutic group in adolescence: A process of intersubjectivation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The therapeutic group in adolescence: A process of intersubjectivation

T. Guénoun and P. Attigui

P. Attigui

Lyon
France

P. Attigui  

Adolescence, group processes, therapeutic psychoanalytic group, process of subjectivation, intersubjectivation

This article analyses group processes in adolescence through therapeutic group interventions. The authors propose to analyse the group aspect, cross-identifications and projective identifications of the adolescent subjectivation process. In which ways does the adolescent subjectivation process need to go through a process of intersubjectivation? This involves a consideration of observable intersubjectivity in a therapeutic group with troubled adolescents. After redefining the notion of subjectivation process and its various manifestations in adolescent psychopathology, the authors further analyse the complex implications of this notion, from the point of view of object cathexes and of links to others. The task is to establish a metapsychology of links to introduce therapeutic psychoanalytic groups into psychic dynamics: in particular, the authors discuss the methodology of theatre-mediated therapeutic psychoanalytic groups, from which the clinical vignette discussed is taken. A case study of a group of adolescents discovers the haphazardness of the link to the other, as a cornerstone towards reinitializing the process of subjectivation that has been hindered in members of the group, and as a way of resuming self-reflectiveness and the reimagining of the self.

Cet article analyse le processus groupal à l’adolescence à partir du dispositif thérapeutique de groupe à médiation. Les auteurs proposent de penser la part groupale, d’identification croisées et d’identifications projectives, du processus de subjectivation adolescent. En quoi le processus de subjectivation adolescent doit en passer par un processus d’inter-subjectivation avec d’autres? Il s’agit de considérer l’intersubjectivité observable en groupe thérapeutique avec adolescents en mal-être. Après une redéfinition de la notion de processus de subjectivation et ses aléas dans la psychopathologie adolescente, les auteurs analysent plus avant les implications complexes de cette notion, du point de vue des investissements pulsionnels objectaux et des liens aux autres. Il s’agit de dresser une métapsychologie des liens introduisant aux enjeux psychiques des groupes thérapeutiques psychanalytiques. Ils exposent plus particulièrement la méthodologie des groupes thérapeutiques à médiation par le théâtre, cadre duquel la vignette clinique exposée est issue. Un cas de groupe d’adolescents permet la mise en évidence de la découverte de l’aléatoire du lien à l’autre par les adolescents, comme une pierre angulaire de la reprise du processus de subjectivation entravé des membres du groupe, et comme modalité de reprise d’un rapport réflexif différenciant, dans un imaginaire de soi à soi.

Dieser Beitrag analysiert Gruppenprozesse in der Adoleszenz anhand therapeutischer Gruppeninterventionen. Die Autoren schlagen vor, den gruppendynamischen Aspekt sowie Kreuzidentifikationen und projektive Identifizierungen im Subjektivierungsprozess der Adoleszenz zu untersuchen. Inwieweit muss der Subjektivierungsprozess von Jugendlichen einen Prozess der Intersubjektivierung durchlaufen? Hierbei erfolgt die Betrachtung der wahrnehmbaren Intersubjektivität in einer Therapiegruppe mit gestörten Jugendlichen. Nach einer Neudefinition des Begriffs des Subjektivierungsprozesses und dessen vielfältigen Erscheinungsformen in der Psychopathologie von Jugendlichen untersuchen die Autoren darüber hinaus die komplexen Implikationen dieses Begriffs aus dem Blickwinkel der Objektbesetzungen und den Bindungen zu anderen Menschen. Ziel ist es, eine Metapsychologie der Bindungen zu begründen, um die analytische Gruppenpsychotherapie in psychische Dynamiken einzuführen: In diesem Zusammenhang greifen die Autoren insbesondere die Methodik der Theatermediation bei analytischer Gruppenpsychotherapie heraus, in der auch die im Beitrag besprochene klinische Vignette ihren Ursprung hat. Eine Fallstudie einer Gruppe von Jugendlichen enthüllt die Zufälligkeit der Bindung zum Anderen als einen der Grundsteine für ein Wiederaufleben des Subjektivierungsprozesses, der bei den Gruppenmitgliedern behindert wurde – und als Weg einer Wiederaufnahme der Selbstreflexion und einer neuen Vorstellung vom Selbst.

El presente artículo analiza los procesos grupales en la adolescencia a través de intervenciones terapéuticas en grupos. Los autores proponen analizar el aspecto grupal, de identificaciones cruzadas e identificaciones proyectivas, en el proceso de subjetivación adolescente. ¿De qué manera el proceso de subjetivación adolescente requiere pasar por un proceso de intersubjetivación? Esto implica considerar la intersubjetividad observable en un grupo terapéutico con adolescentes perturbados. Luego de redefinir la noción de proceso de subjetivación y sus variadas manifestaciones en la psicopatología adolescente, los autores analizan más a fondo las complejas implicaciones de tal noción, desde el punto de vista de las catexias objetales y los vínculos con otros. Se trata de establecer una metapsicología de los vínculos para introducir a los grupos psicoanalíticos terapéuticos en dinámicas psíquicas: analizan, en particular, la metodología de los grupos psicoanaliticos terapéuticos a través del teatro, de donde se extrae la viñeta clínica presentada. Un estudio de caso sobre un grupo de adolescentes descubre la aleatoriedad del vínculo con el otro como piedra angular para el reinicio del proceso de subjetivación que había estado trabado en los miembros del grupo, y como una forma de reanudar la reflexión sobre sí mismo y la reimaginación del self.

[[RunningHead]]

Contributors and Reviewers for 2021

The Editors, Dana Birksted-Breen, Francis Grier, Bruce Reis, Bernard Reith, Beatriz de León de Bernardi, Georg Bruns, and Catherine Humble would like to express their gratitude to the team of reviewers throughout the world responsible for the successful implementation of the Journal’s peer-review process. These colleagues devote a great deal of time and effort in the service of providing detailed and thoughtful evaluations of manuscripts, film essays and book reviews which will ultimately help the authors strengthen their contributions.

We would also like to thank the section editors for all their hard work: Heinz Weiss (Education Section), Rachel Blass (Psychoanalytic Controversies), Abbot Bronstein (Analyst at Work), Caroline Bainbridge and Joan Thompson (Film Essays), Emily Alster (Book Reviews), Bruce Reis (North American Book Reviews), and all our Book Review Associates. We would also like to thank their Editorial teams.

Without the dedication of the section editors and their teams, along with the reviewers, the IJP would not have achieved the scholarly status it enjoys, and we would like to thank them for everything they have done to assist us during the past 12 months.

In addition, we would like to thank the editors and board members of the IJP Annuals who produce collections of IJP papers in various languages. We wish to express our appreciation to our translators who carry out essential work for the Journal’s aims. Last but not least, we would like to thank Chris Lawrence our Journal Assistant for his hard work behind the scenes. We thank the following reviewers:

Nicola Abel-Hirsch

Adela Abella

Jan Abram

Silvia Acosta

Sarah Ackerman

Joseph Aguayo

responseSet.title: COVID-19 IN V ACTS
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

COVID-19 IN V ACTS

Piotr Krzakowski

Piotr Krzakowski

France

Piotr Krzakowski   CONTACT Piotr Krzakowski krzakowski.piotr@gmail.com
COVID - Remote sessions, psychoanalytical setting, psychoanalytical process, maternal factor of the session, working groups

This article follows almost chronologically the COVID crisis between March and May 2020 during what is called, at least in Europe, the “first wave”. Each ‘Act’ of our internal and external theatre is therefore a moment with a specific date, with the questions that were then pertinent. These ‘Acts’ were:

First was the setting up of remote sessions under health pressures and the recommendations of our psychoanalytic institutions. This change in the frame and its consequences will be presented from various technical points of view, which have ostensibly raised some original metapsychological hypotheses.

Then, concerning our profession, its very status as either essential or inessential has been discussed by public authorities, and inevitably by our patients, who will après-coup have to give meaning to our reactions during this crisis.

We will next study the effects of remote sessions, particularly from its psychoanalytic ‘economic’ perspective, and as a kind of ‘credit for in-presence’ in the early stages of quarantine.

We will then be looking at the hypothesis of a maternal element in the sessions, imperceptible in normal times, but suddenly palpable in the context of the absence of physical bodies.

Finally, we will propose developments through workshops as an option in order to find a response to this unexpected event at the global scale.

responseSet.title: Summary page, issue 3, 2021
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Summary page, issue 3, 2021

Letter From

Ahead of the Vancouver 2021 IPA Congress (this year held virtually), Endre Koritar provides a brief history of the development of psychoanalysis in Canada and the formation of the Canadian Society.

Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique

The words uttered by the analytic couple can be thought of as the text in which the mind attempts to describe what is already occurring musically at the level of the drives and unconscious phantasy, writes Francis Grier in his examination of the musical countertransference.

Transgender is rooted in the infant’s early discovery of gender difference and how the primary identification is processed or “translated” in the après-coup, writes Susann Heenen-Wolff. She identifies the formation of the “body ego” as the result of primary identifications and the “sexual ego,” which stems from secondary identifications.

Reflecting on the ethical status of psychoanalysis, Jérôme Glas argues that the ethical specificity of psychoanalysis is determined in particular by the transference. He asks: what happens when the analyst is deprived of his ethical status as recipient of the transference and becomes an ordinary object in the field of everyday objectality?

A large and significant portion of contemporary psychoanalytic theory has given up on the drives. Although there are legitimate criticisms, the drives require careful examination, argues Jordan Conrad. Providing an account of the drives informed by German and English thought, Conrad distinguishes between “drive” and its conceptual neighbour “instinct.”

Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis

Tamara Guenon looks at adolescent subjectivation processes in relation to object cathexes and links to others. In order to aid therapeutic groups for adolescents Guenon attempts to establish a metapsychology of links. She goes on to discuss theatre-mediated therapeutic groups.

Rosely Lerner looks at the precocious origin of perverse behaviour. Situations of humiliation and exposure to adult sexuality, creating premature eroticization, are central to the development of patterns of behaviour that may become organised as a perverse structure in adult age, Lerner argues. 441

responseSet.title: Reply to the letter by Michel Sanchez-Cardenas: Does the repetition compulsion really have a purpose?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to the letter by Michel Sanchez-Cardenas: Does the repetition compulsion really have a purpose?

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Stuttgart
Germany

Frankurt a.M.
Germany

Heinz Weiss   CONTACT Heinz Weiss weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de Abteilung für Psychosomatische Medizin, Robert-Bosch-Krankenhaus, Auerbachstrasse 110, D-70376 Stuttgart; Sigmund-Freud-Institut, Myliusstrasse 20, Frankurt a.M. D-60323

I would like to thank Michel Sanchez-Cardenas for his thoughtful comments on our section on the repetition compulsion. In addition to the hypothesis presented by Marilia Aisenstein, Rachel Blass, Howard Levine and myself, he discusses the possibility that the repetition compulsion might not have a “purpose” or meaning at all, except repetition itself.

Seeing repetition as a “primum movens in and by itself” is, of course, what we encounter clinically when we are faced with stuck or “endless” patients. As Michel Sanchez-Cardenas points out, the compulsion to repeat may originate from a relational pattern that was formed by the key figures of our childhood or the key figures that have been built up in our internal word, like, for instance, a punitive superego. However, I would like to add that these relational patterns are not simply repetitions of what we have experienced, but are based on complex identifications, and that those “key figures” have been built up by cycles of projection and introjection, which themselves can become repetitive and then constantly “play the same ‘film’”, as Sanchez-Cardenas says. We may see those “films” as replicative attachment patterns, or as an attempt to stabilize a fragile identity, as is the case in some borderline and psychotic individuals. Then it may appear better “to repeat what we know, even if it is painful” (Sanchez-Cardenas 2021, 1008).

I am not sure, however, whether it makes sense to extend the notion of repetition as far as comprising the invariant parts of the personality, in particular the most primitive ones. In any case, we should not obliterate the difference between repetition and the repetition compulsion.

Repetition is an ubiquitous phenomenon that is indispensable to learn from experience and therefore for psychic development. It plays an essential role in play, identification and creative imagination. Anthropologists like Buytendijk (1933) and Huizinga (1938) have highlighted the importance of playful repetition in role-play and symbol-formation – the first with extensive reference to Freud. In philosophy, it was Soren Kierkegaard (1843) who emphasized that repetition never returns to the same point, but always recaptures something new and thus lends our experiences liveliness and vitality. The dialectic function of repetition as linking the past with the present and anticipating future experiences is a central feature in Edmund Husserl’s analysis of time, in particular in his late genetic phenomenology (Husserl 1938; see Brudzinska 2021). Repetition plays an 1011

responseSet.title: Response to Michel Sanchez-Cardenas’ letter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Michel Sanchez-Cardenas’ letter

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

Jerusalem, Israel

London, UK

Rachel Blass   CONTACT Rachel Blass rachelblass@gmail.com

All human behaviour and experience can be studied and explained at different levels, including social, political, biological, chemical, physical and psychological. Within each level there can be further subdivisions: the person's behaviour can be explained neurologically and hormonally, in terms of evolution and class theories, behaviourism or psychoanalysis, etc.

At the start of his letter regarding the series of papers on repetition compulsion that appeared in a previous issue of the IJP (2020, 101:1162–1214), Sanchez-Cardenas claims that he will briefly review the papers and then offer an additional hypothesis. In practice, however, what he does is this: he sums up the papers in a line or two, stressing their concern with motives and meanings, especially conflictual ones; and then he turns to present a number of additional hypotheses of his own that do not have this concern, but rather regard repetition as purposeless and meaningless, non-psychical “automatisms”. The brevity and specific focus of the summaries of the earlier series of papers limit and distort their content, and the presentation of the additional hypotheses misleadingly suggests that they are relevant to psychoanalytic discourse. They are not. In eschewing meaning, purpose, motive and conflict, in turning away from what is precisely psychical – all defining features of psychoanalytic explanation – Sanchez-Cardenas’ explanations are essentially non-analytic. That is, the explanations he offers may be valid, but they are from learning theory, attachment theory, neuropsychology and non-analytic theories of culture and cognition. Juxtaposing them with quotations from Bion and Ferro, or putting them under a heading of “psychoanalytic thought”, does not change this fact.

Counter to his explicit intention, at some points he does seem to be speaking of meaning. This is when Sanchez-Cardenas offers the idea that repetition is sought because of the security and limits it provides through familiarity and constancy. But from a psychoanalytic perspective this is a very general and superficial explanation. First, it is in line with the pleasure principle and the kind of explanations explored by Freud as he tried to argue against his own hypothesis of there being not only instances of actual repetition, but also a special compulsion to repeat, a compulsion defined by the fact that it yields no pleasure. Second, when one wonders why some people, more than others, are prone at times to seek familiarity and thus security through repetition, and why the repetitions are of some things and not others, one is forced to move beyond this general level of explanation to deeper, unconscious analytic ones of the kinds found in the earlier series of papers.

responseSet.title: Obituary: Anton Kris
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary: Anton Kris

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

Newton
MA
USA

Judy Kantrowitz   CONTACT Judy Kantrowitz judy_kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu 1501 Beacon street, Apt 503, Brookline, MA 02446, USA

Tony Kris, scholar, clinician, mentor and friend, died on 11 March 2021. When you would see Tony and in greeting him ask, “How are you?”, his standard reply was, “Could be worse, probably will be.” Although this was his jocular response, he had good reason for his caution. His father, Ernst Kris, the renowned ego psychologist–psychoanalyst, had had his first cardiac event at the age of 40 while monitoring German army radio dispatches in Great Britain in 1940. He had heard that the Germans had broken through the Maginot Line and understood the increased danger to the Allies. His father survived, but he died of a heart attack at the age of 57. Tony was convinced his fate would be the same. At the age of 40, while my husband and I were visiting Tony and his family at their summer home in Martha’s Vineyard, he had a small but alarming heart attack. He was treated successfully. At the age of 50, he had a triple bypass. Over the next two decades, he was to experience a variety of heart problems and during the last decade of his life underwent numerous cardiac procedures.

Unfortunately, during the last year and half of his life, he was subject to a variety of heart-related health difficulties, including kidney failure, which he originally assumed was a primary disorder. His sister, Anna Kris Wolff, had been on dialysis for over a year before her death. Tony had devotedly tended to her care. He was clear he did not want to live like that, or die like that. He planned for his retirement at the end of June 2021. Then the doctors and he began to consider that this kidney problem was likely related to his heart condition. An aortic valve replacement could likely fix this problem. So Tony became optimistic that his kidney difficulty would be reversed and he told patients and supervisees and all of us that he would likely not have to retire after all. On the day before the procedure, Tony was optimistic about his future. He had survived so many heart operations before. “Talk to you tomorrow night”, he said the day before his operation. Not a bad way to die, but for us, his family, friends, and colleagues still way too soon.

responseSet.title: A people’s history of psychoanalysis: from Freud to liberation psychology: by Daniel José Gaztambide, Lanham, Lexington Books, 2019, 231 pp., £31 (hardcopy), ISBN: 9781498565745
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A people’s history of psychoanalysis: from Freud to liberation psychology

by Daniel José Gaztambide, Lanham, Lexington Books, 2019, 231 pp., £31 (hardcopy), ISBN: 9781498565745

Yukari Yanagino

Yukari Yanagino

New York
NY

Yukari Yanagino   yyanagio@gmail.com

Daniel Gaztambide's People's History of Psychoanalysis takes the reader on a journey that starts from nineteenth-century Vienna and goes around the globe to Africa, North America and South America. It is a journey that traces Freud's influence on radical thinkers who were instrumental in sociopolitical movements to emancipate the oppressed in their respective nations. Contrary to the assumption that psychoanalysis is a clinical practice that is only pertinent to the white and wealthy, Gaztambide argues that Freud's work had a global impact on thinkers who were engaged in social justice work to radically alter oppressive societal structures. He writes that psychoanalysis has been instrumental to emancipatory work. It allowed the analysis of oppression while Lanham and Boulder seeking to “develop strategies and methodologies for reconciliation with, and resistance and rebellion against, Whiteness” (116). Throughout the book he sustains his argument that psychoanalysis can point out the distortions hegemonic structure produces, which condone violence against the Other for the purpose of maintaining power.

Although Gaztambide introduces many thinkers in his work, he is mainly interested in linking Freud with two other important thinkers: Franz Fanon and Paulo Freire. He adds the work of Ignacio Martín-Baró, who was instrumental in the creation of liberation psychology. Gaztambide argues that “Latin America stands at a crossroads of time and space, the result of a five-hundred-year-old wound that brought together European colonizers, captured Black people sold into slavery, and the indigenous people who inhabited this land long before” (119). Reading this I did wonder about the omission from his analysis of the massive immigration of Asians that changed the economic structures of Latin American countries. This wave of immigration was in the wake of the worldwide development of capitalism and imperialism in the nineteenth century contemporaneous to the decline and, finally, the abolishment of slavery in Latin America. The bulk of early Chinese and Japanese immigrants were introduced as agricultural labours, working alongside Africans who were brought as slaves. When slavery was finally abolished in Peru in 1854, Chinese immigrants supplemented African slave labour in plantation-based economies and the construction of railroads throughout Latin America.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Threshold of Loss and its Representations: Masochism as a Safeguard against Depressive Disorder

Elise Pelladeau

Elise Pelladeau

0000-0001-6736-9767
CAPS, Poitiers, France
elise.pelladeau@gmail.com

Elise Pelladeau  

Melancholy, defence, depression, masochism, idealization

This article explores defensive modes of masochism based through the case study of Sarah, a 25-year-old patient who is regularly followed in psychoanalytical therapy. I will postulate the idea that fetishization of moral masochism should be seen from the perspective of melancholic processes. Drawing on several moments of her therapy, I will illustrate how the primitive idealization of the introjected and denigrated object in the melancholic process lays the conditions for a decisive moral masochism, and whose unconscious idealization defends against depressive breakdown.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Encapsulated body engrams and somatic narration - Integrating body memory into psychoanalytic technique

Sebastian Leikert

Sebastian Leikert

Saarbrücken, Germany
s.leikert@web.de
Lindenstrasse 8, D 66128 Saarbrücken, Germany www.sebastian-leikert.de

Sebastian Leikert  

Trauma; technique; body; representability/figurability; countertransference; unconscious

Beginning with the observation that a good integration of soma and psyche is often missing in our work with severely disturbed analysands, two concepts are discussed that focus constellations of bodily encoded unconscious material, which needs a specific form of working-through. The concept of encapsulated body engrams referring to defence structures revolving around the inhibition or disorganization of an affective bodily impulse is outlined. The imbalance arising this way is perceived as a foreign body within the body-self. These engrams are repetitive and are not susceptible to symbolic transformation. Parallels between the encapsulated body engram, the autistic objects (Tustin, F. 1980. “Autistic Objects.” International Review of Psycho-Analysis 27: 27-39) and Freud's theory of inhibition (Freud, S. 1926. “Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety.” SE 20: 77-175) are discussed. To unblock encapsulated engrams, body feelings emerging in the chain of associations are understood as attempts to communicate. In a process called somatic narration these perceptions are contextualized with other bodily sensations. The working-through of this material in transference and countertransference is described in the light of Lombardi's concepts (Lombardi, R. 2017. Body- Mind Dissociation in Psychoanalysis - Development after Bion. New York: Routledge). Two case reports precede the theoretical discussion.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gaze Avoidance in Parent-Infant Psychotherapy: Manifestations and Technical Suggestions

Björn Salomonsson

Björn Salomonsson

0000-0003-4747-541X
Stockholm, Sweden
bjorn.salomonsson@ki.se; bjornsalomonsson50@gmail.com

Björn Salomonsson  

Gaze avoidance; parent-infant psychotherapy; countertransference; psychotherapeutic technique

Infants express emotional distress through whining, crying, flailing, silence, etc., which can be viewed as communications that also afflict the caregiver(s). One expressive mode, subtle and often unnoticed except by the parents, is infant gaze avoidance. It often elicits parental feelings of rejection, shame and despair, and may be a reason for seeking parent-infant psychotherapy. In therapy, the symptom often discloses a disturbance in the dyad’s emotional interaction. Therapy can bring about relief in the symptom and the relational disorder. Sometimes, the therapist discovers that the infant is avoiding the therapist’s, rather than the parents’, eyes. This challenges the therapist’s expertise in establishing contact with the baby and in perceiving and processing emotional reactions to what may be experienced as the baby’s dismissal. Gaze avoidance elicits theoretical questions approached in a previous publication: what does the child seem to avoid in the adult’s eyes, and how can we conceptualize the psychodynamics behind the symptom? It also evokes technical questions: how can the therapist make contact with an infant who avoids the mother’s or the therapist’s eyes? How can the clinician exploit their emotional reactions, the countertransference, to understand and further the dyad’s emotional communication? Two case vignettes are provided.

[[RunningHead]]

Can we think psychoanalytically about transgenderism? An expanded live Zoom debate with David Bell and Avgi Saketopoulou, moderated by Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass , David Bell and Avgi Saketopoulou

Avgi Saketopoulou

Avgi Saketopoulou  

Introduction by Rachel B. Blass

In Issue 5 of last year’s IJP we published a controversial discussion titled: “Can we think psychoanalytically about transgenderism?” The participants were Avgi Saketopoulou, a New York psychoanalyst who has worked extensively with trans adults, trans children, and otherwise gender variant patients in various private and institutional contexts and stages of transitioning and London analyst, David Bell, who in his role as a Staff Governor of the Tavistock and Portman NHS Foundation Trust was approached by a large number of clinicians who were working in the Tavistock Gender Identity Development Service (GIDS). They raised very serious ethical and clinical concerns which formed the basis of his subsequent report which was part of the chain of events leading to the Judicial Review which found that children and young people lacked the capacity to consent to this treatment. Since then he has researched the area of gender dysphoria in children. The controversy included a paper from each of these analysts. We reserved for a later time the discussion of each other’s papers, normally included within the same issue. We are now publishing this discussion, which was held in a new and very different format, one which we thought would be helpful to the elucidation of this complex issue. Rather than inviting written responses we invited the two authors to an exchange within the context of a live Zoom meeting, with me (R. B.) as moderator. We had originally planned to publish the transcription of the live meeting with minor editorial modifications in order to avoid redundancy, for purposes of confidentiality, and to allow for focusing of the issues. To facilitate the last aim we also added headings. But in the process of the approving the edited transcription it was greatly expanded. Additions by one discussant in the effort to present fuller and more accurate positions required response by the other and ultimately a further series of written exchanges was blended into the original live one. As will be seen agreement was not at all reached, and thus it seemed that the back and forth could go on endlessly. Avgi had begun the cycle of additions and so she kindly offered David the opportunity to conclude it (with his final comments appearing in an addendum).

responseSet.title: The music of the drives, and the music of perversion: reflections on a dream of jealous theft
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The music of the drives, and the music of perversion: reflections on a dream of jealous theft

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

London
UK

Francis Grier   CONTACT Francis Grier fjrgrier@gmail.com
Perverse, musicality, unconscious phantasy

I investigate the possibility that in order to reach truly disturbed parts of the patient's mind, the analyst also may need to mobilise similar areas in his own psyche. I examine the part musicality might contribute to this well-known idea, that is, I consider what is set off in the analyst's mind by the changing qualities of the actual music of the analytic couple's voices, and also by the musical events that occur in response in the analyst's mind. The term perversion is particularly used to explore the perverse qualities of the musical countertransference. I propose that these primal musical processes are ubiquitous, taking place at a fundamental psychic level before images or words are formed. I re-examine Isaacs' (1948) theory that the images of unconscious phantasy are the most archaic expression of the drives, and suggest that the music of unconscious phantasy may assert its prior claim in this regard, the infant's ability to form images deriving from this prior musical capacity. I propose that the words – a much later development – that are then uttered by the analytic couple can be thought of as the text in which the mind attempts to describe what is already occurring musically at the level of the drives and unconscious phantasy. I suggest that many operas offer a paradigm of this process, and offer a helpful way for analysts to think about verbal communication, both interpersonal and intrapsychic.

L'auteur de cet article examine l'hypothèse suivante : afin de parvenir à atteindre véritablement les parties perturbées du psychisme d'un patient, il se pourrait que l'analyste ait besoin de mobiliser certaines zones similaires de son propre psychisme. Il étudie la contribution possible de la musicalité à ce fait bien connu, à savoir : ce qui est déclenché dans le psychisme de l'analyste par les qualités changeantes de la musique des voix du couple analytique et également par les manifestations musicales qui y font écho dans l'esprit de l'analyste. Il fait appel à la notion de perversion pour explorer les qualités perverses du contre-transfert musical. Il considère que ces processus musicaux primitifs sont omniprésents et qu'ils se produisent à un niveau psychique fondamental, avant que les images ou les mots ne soient formés. Il revisite la théorie de Susan Isaacs (1948) selon laquelle les images des fantasmes inconscients sont l'expression la plus archaïque des pulsions, et émet l'idée que la musique du fantasme inconscient peut revendiquer son antériorité à cet égard, la capacité du nourrisson de former des images tirant son origine de cette aptitude musicale préexistante. L'auteur propose que les mots – un développement bien plus tardif – qui sont ensuite prononcés par le couple analytique, puissent être considérés comme le texte par le biais duquel le psychisme tenterait de décrire ce qui existerait déjà musicalement, au niveau des pulsions et du fantasme inconscient. Enfin, l'auteur laisse entendre que de nombreux opéras apparaissent comme un paradigme de ce processus et offrent aux analystes un moyen utile, qui leur permet de penser la communication verbale, interpersonnelle et intrapsychique.

Ich befasse mich mit der Möglichkeit, dass ein Analytiker möglicherweise ähnliche Bereiche in seiner eigenen Psyche mobilisieren muss, um sehr gestörte Anteile im Geist des Patienten zu erreichen. Ich untersuche, inwieweit Musikalität bei dieser hinreichend bekannten Überlegung mitwirken könnte, sprich, ich betrachte, was mental beim Analytiker durch sich verändernde Klangfarben der von den Stimmen des analytischen Paares erzeugten Musik und auch durch die musikalischen Vorgänge ausgelöst wird, die als geistige Reaktion des Analytikers auftreten. Der Begriff Perversion wird insbesondere verwendet, um die perversen Eigenschaften der musikalischen Gegenübertragung zu untersuchen. Ich stelle den Gedanken in den Raum, dass diese urmusikalischen Abläufe allgegenwärtig sind und auf der elementaren psychischen Ebene ablaufen, bevor Bilder oder Wörter gebildet werden. Ich überprüfe Susan Isaacs Theorie aus dem Jahr 1948, die besagt, dass die Bilder der unbewussten Fantasie die ursprünglichste Ausdrucksform der Triebe seien und lege nahe, dass die Musik der unbewussten Fantasie möglicherweise so ihren Erstanspruch geltend macht – sich die Fähigkeit des Säuglings, Bilder zu entwickeln, aus dieser vorherigen musikalischen Fähigkeit ableiten lässt. Meiner Auffassung nach können die sehr viel später entstehenden Wörter, die dann von dem analytischen Paar geäußert werden, als der Text gedacht werden, mit dem der Geist versucht zu beschreiben, was sich musikalisch bereits auf der Ebene der Triebe und der unbewussten Fantasie ereignet. Ich bringe vor, dass viele Opern ein Paradigma für diesen Prozess sein können und so für Analytiker ein nützliches Medium sind, um über verbale interpersonale und intrapsychische Kommunikation nachzudenken.

In questo lavoro esaminerò l'ipotesi per cui, al fine di raggiungere le parti più disturbate della mente dei pazienti, anche l'analista debba mettere in gioco aree simili della sua stessa psiche. In particolare mi concentrerò sul ruolo che, rispetto a questa idea assai diffusa, potrebbe avere la musicalità – e nel farlo considererò innanzitutto che cos'è che si attiva nella mente dell'analista laddove la qualità della musica creata dalle voci della coppia analitica cambia, e che cosa è attivato dagli eventi di natura musicale che, in risposta a tali cambiamenti, si producono nella mente dell'analista. Il termine 'perversione' verrà qui chiamato in causa in modo particolare per esplorare le qualità perverse del controtransfert musicale. La mia ipotesi è che i processi musicali primari a cui mi riferisco siano ubiqui e abbiano luogo a un livello di base della psiche: un livello che precede quello della formazione di immagini e parole. Riesaminerò a questo proposito la teoria di Isaacs (1948) secondo cui le immagini delle fantasie inconsce rappresenterebbero l'espressione più arcaica delle pulsioni, suggerendo per parte mia a tale riguardo che è invece la musica ad avere la priorità: il neonato deriverebbe infatti la sua capacità di formare immagini precisamente da questa sua precedente dote musicale. Sosterrò inoltre che le parole (che emergono decisamente più tardi nello sviluppo) pronunciate dalla coppia analitica possono essere pensate come il testo tramite cui la mente prova a descrivere ciò che già sta accadendo su un piano musicale al livello delle pulsioni e della fantasia inconscia. In molte opere si possono cogliere esempi di questo processo: esempi che offrono agli analisti un utile strumento per pensare alla comunicazione verbale, sia interpersonale sia intrapsichica.

En el presente artículo se investiga la posibilidad de que, para llegar a partes verdaderamente perturbadas de la mente del paciente, el analista requiera también movilizar áreas similares en su propia psiquis. Se examina el papel que la musicalidad desempeñaría en esta bien conocida idea, es decir, se examina aquello que la cualidad cambiante de la música real de las voces de la pareja analítica desencadena en la mente del analista, y también los hechos musicales que ocurren en la mente del analista en respuesta a ello. Se utiliza el término perversión en particular para explorar las cualidades perversas de la contratransferencia musical. Se propone que estos procesos musicales primarios son ubicuos y ocurren en un nivel psíquico fundamental, previo a la formación de las imágenes o las palabras. Se reexamina la teoría de Isaacs (1948) de que las imágenes de la fantasía inconsciente son la expresión más arcaica de las pulsiones, y se sugiere que la música de la fantasía inconsciente puede reivindicar su carácter previo a este respecto: la capacidad del bebé de formar imágenes se deriva de esta capacidad musical previa. Se propone que las palabras –un desarrollo muy posterior– pronunciadas por la pareja analítica pueden ser consideradas como el texto en el que la mente intenta describir lo que ya está ocurriendo musicalmente en el nivel de las pulsiones y de la fantasía inconsciente. Se sugiere que muchas óperas brindan un paradigma de este proceso y ofrecen una forma útil para que los analistas piensen sobre la comunicación verbal, tanto interpersonal como intrapsíquica.

responseSet.title: The Sense of the Past: Theoretical and Clinical Aspects of Deferred Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sense of the Past: Theoretical and Clinical Aspects of Deferred Action

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3203-3494
sgroarke2@gmail.com

Steven Groarke  

Construction; conviction; memory; not-yet-conceived object; reclamation; unlived experience

Psychoanalysis has made a singular contribution to our understanding of how we live with the past; most importantly, the concept of “deferred action” (it will become clear why I wish to retain the English translation) challenges the association of causality with a particular temporal direction, that is, the earlier-to-later direction of causal efficacy. The purpose of this paper is to explore the implications of “backwards causation” (as it were) based on an application of the theory of object-use to the problem of memory. I begin with some introductory comments on Winnicott’s theory of object-use, before presenting clinical observations on a patient’s emerging sense of the past. In the theoretical discussion that follows, I consider the problem of memory in light of the clinical example. A discussion of the continued importance of “deferred action” is presented together with the introduction of the concept of “reclamation” as a type of re-descriptive memory.

responseSet.title: High-risk sexual behaviours, from the neurotica to complex trauma: Psychopathologies of repetition
responseSet.authorMast: Marion Robin,  Aziz Essadek,  Maurice Corcos and  Gérard Shadili
responseSet.authorCitation: Robin, M., Essadek, A., Corcos, M. & Shadili, G.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

High-risk sexual behaviours, from the neurotica to complex trauma: Psychopathologies of repetition

Marion Robin , Aziz Essadek , Maurice Corcos and Gérard Shadili

Gérard Shadili

Paris
France

Nancy
France

Paris
France

Gérard Shadili  

Adolescents, borderline personality, high-risk sexual behaviours, complex trauma

In adolescent psychiatry, when patients present high-risk sexual behaviours, analysing the symptoms usually leads back to a traumatic sexual event in childhood. These clinical situations include a first stage in which the trauma is constituted and a second adolescent stage in which the mnemic trace can lead to the traumatic experience being reproduced within a destructured psychic apparatus that is seeking restructuration through actions. These two psychopathological stages are examined in the light of the connection between Freud’s two paradigms of the neurotica and the theory of fantasy, focusing therapeutically either on the primacy of sexual abuse or on the psychic representation of a traumatic sexuality, respectively. Here, the elaboration of these behaviours relates either to the individual traumatic history or the transgenerational history, or to both histories, intermingled or even undifferentiated. The repetition processes and the temporary loss of reality-testing in these clinical situations are analysed there, including by the yardstick of the psychopathology of complex trauma, as well as by that of containment in the familial and therapeutic environment, from which the subject will benefit in the context of the revelation processes.

Les conduites sexuelles à risque, de la neurotica au trauma complexe : psychopathologies de la répétition. En psychiatrie, lorsque des adolescents présentent des conduites sexuelles à risque, le fil d'analyse du symptôme permet le plus souvent de remonter à un événement traumatique sexuel de l'enfance. Ces situations cliniques incluent un premier temps infantile de constitution du trauma, et un deuxième temps adolescent où la trace mnésique peut amener à reproduire l'expérience traumatique au sein d'un psychisme déjà déstructuré, l'appelant ainsi à se restructurer par les actes. Ces deux temps psychopathologiques sont examinés à la lumière du lien entre les deux paradigmes freudiens de la neurotica puis de la théorie du fantasme, plaçant respectivement le focus thérapeutique sur le primat de l'abus sexuel, ou bien sur la représentation psychique d'une sexualité traumatique. Ici, l'élaboration de ces comportements renvoie à l'histoire traumatique individuelle ou bien à l'histoire transgénérationnelle, ou encore aux deux histoires entremêlées voire indifférenciées. Les processus de répétition et la perte transitoire de l'épreuve de réalité dans ces situations cliniques y sont analysés, entre autres, à l'aune de la psychopathologie du trauma complexe, mais aussi à celle de la contenance environnementale, familiale et thérapeutique, dont le sujet va bénéficier dans le cadre des processus de révélation.

Risikoreiche sexuelle Verhaltensweisen, von derNeurotica zum komplexen Trauma: Psychopathologien der Wiederholung. Wenn Patienten in der Adoleszenzpsychiatrie risikoreiche sexuelle Verhaltensweisen zeigen, führt die Analyse der Symptome in der Regel zu einem traumatischen sexuellen Ereignis in der Kindheit zurück. Zu diesen klinischen Situationen gehört eine erste infantile Phase, in der das Trauma entsteht, und eine zweite adoleszente Phase, in der die mnestische Spur dazu führen kann, dass die traumatische Erfahrung in einem destrukturierten psychischen Apparat reproduziert wird, der Restrukturierung durch Handlungen anstrebt. Diese beiden psychopathologischen Phasen werden vor dem Hintergrund der Verbindung zwischen Freuds zwei Paradigmen der Neurotica und der Theorie der Fantasie betrachtet, wobei man sich therapeutisch jeweils entweder auf das Primat des sexuellen Missbrauchs oder auf die psychische Repräsentation einer traumatischen Sexualität konzentriert. Hier bezieht sich die Entwicklung dieser Verhaltensweisen entweder auf die individuelle traumatische Geschichte oder die transgenerationale Geschichte, oder beide Geschichten, die entweder miteinander vermischt oder sogar vollkommen undifferenziert sind. Die Wiederholungsprozesse und der zeitweilige Verlust der Realitätsprüfung in diesen klinischen Situationen werden dort analysiert, auch mit dem Maßstab der Psychopathologie des komplexen Traumas sowie des Containments im familiären und therapeutischen Umfeld, wovon das Subjekt im Rahmen der Enthüllungsprozesse profitieren wird.

Le condotte sessuali a rischio, dai 'neurotica' al trauma complesso: psicopatologie della ripetizione. In psichiatria, quando gli adolescenti presentano delle condotte sessuali a rischio l'analisi del sintomo permette solitamente di risalire a un evento traumatico di natura sessuale occorso durante l'infanzia. Questo tipo di situazioni cliniche consta di una prima fase, in cui il trauma viene a costituirsi durante l'infanzia, e di una seconda fase in cui nel corso dell'adolescenza la traccia mnestica può portare a riprodurre l'esperienza traumatica all'interno di un apparato psichico destrutturato che cerca di ristrutturarsi attraverso il ricorso all'azione. Queste due fasi psicopatologiche vengono qui esaminate alla luce del legame tra i due paradigmi freudiani dei 'neurotica' e della teoria della fantasia, che da un punto di vista terapeutico considerano prioritari rispettivamente l'evento dell'abuso sessuale e la rappresentazione psichica di una sessualità traumatica. L'elaborazione di questi comportamenti riconduce qui a storie individuali traumatiche, o alla storia transgenerazionale, o magari a entrambi i tipi di storia mescolati tra loro e finanche indifferenziati. I processi di ripetizione e la temporanea perdita dell'esame di realtà che caratterizzano questi quadri clinici verranno qui analizzati alla luce della psicopatologia del trauma complesso, ma anche del contenimento offerto dall'ambiente, dalla famiglia e dal setting terapeutico - un contenimento da cui il paziente potrà trarre beneficio durante il processo di rivelazione.

La conducta sexual de alto riesgo, de la neurótica al trauma complejo: psicopatologías de la repetición. Cuando en la psiquiatría de adolescentes los pacientes presentan conductas sexuales de alto riesgo, el análisis de los síntomas suele remontarse a un acontecimiento sexual traumático en la infancia. Estas situaciones clínicas incluyen una primera etapa infantil en la que se constituye el trauma, y una segunda etapa adolescente donde la huella mnémica puede llevar a reproducir la experiencia traumática en el aparato psíquico desestructurado, en busca de una reestructuración mediante actos. Se examinan estas dos etapas psicopatológicas a la luz del vínculo entre los paradigmas de la neurótica y de la teoría de la fantasía de Freud, centrándose terapéuticamente en la primacía del abuso sexual o en la representación psíquica de una sexualidad traumática, respectivamente. Aquí, la elaboración de estas conductas se relaciona con la historia traumática individual o con la historia transgeneracional, o con ambas historias, entremezcladas o, incluso, indiferenciadas. Se analizan los procesos de repetición y la pérdida temporal de la prueba de realidad en estas situaciones clínicas, tanto en términos de la psicopatología del trauma complejo, como en el de la contención en el entorno familiar y terapéutico, de la cual el sujeto se beneficiará en el contexto de los procesos de revelación.

responseSet.title: The infantile: Which meaning?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The infantile: Which meaning?

Jorge Canestri

Jorge Canestri

Rome
Italy

Jorge Canestri   CONTACT Jorge Canestri canestrijorge@gmail.com Italian Psychoanalytical Association, Rome, Italy
Infantile, transference, Freud

The “in-fans”, who cannot speak, needs a narrator, especially a psychoanalyst in the transference in order to be able to hear again the “silent” language that is expressed in the time of the “Jetztzeit”. Through a reflection on Freud's work on aphasias and his exchange of letters with Fliess, it is possible to identify important previews which constitute a sort of pre-semiotics, as well as a theory on memory. In addition, history, sociology, semiotics and the neurosciences are all necessary interlocutors for developing the issue of The Infantile in psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: The Conceptualization of Trauma in Psychoanalysis: An Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Conceptualization of Trauma in Psychoanalysis: An Introduction

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de
Myliusstrasse 20, D-60323 Frankfurt a.M

Heinz Weiss  

Conceptualization of trauma; external/internal reality; traumatic defensive organizations; repetition compulsion; reparation; guilt

This paper highlights some aspects of the conceptualization of trauma in psychoanalysis and introduces the three subsequent papers by Bernard Chervet, Jan Abrams and Howard Levine. It focuses on the interchange between external and internal reality, the construction of traumatic defensive organizations and the role of reparation and guilt in overcoming the repetition compulsion.

responseSet.title: Enactment: A necessary conceptual review
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Enactment: A necessary conceptual review

Valeria Corbella

Valeria Corbella

Valeria Corbella   valeriacorbella@yahoo.com.ar
Enactment, interdiscipline, acting out, pluralism, epistemology

Enactment as a psychoanalytic concept was coined by Jacobs in 1986. Since then, it has developed within the psychoanalytic community as a concept. Originally, enactment described an aspect of countertransference expressed through subtle actions within the analytic process, as distinct from noisier performances that fall within the spectrum of “acting out”. Nowadays, the concept of enactment poses some difficulties that should not be ignored. As a theoretical concept, enactment has a complex epistemological position, and as a clinical concept it sheds light on contemporary psychoanalytic practices. This paper explores the results from an empirical and conceptual research work, focusing on the history, translations and usages of the term “enactment”, and its comparison with related terms. It will also consider the epistemological perspective regarding the current status of enactment as a scientific concept and its relationship with the psychoanalytic theoretical field.

La mise en acte comme concept psychanalytique a été développé par Jacobs en 1986. Depuis lors, elle a évolué en tant que concept au sein de la communauté psychanalytique au large. À l'origine, mise en acte décrivait un aspect du contre-transfert exprimé par des actions subtiles dans le processus analytique, distinct des performances plus démonstratives décrites par la catégorie « passage à l’acte ». De nos jours, le concept de mise en acte pose certaines difficultés à ne pas ignorer. Et en tant que concept théorique, la mise en acte a une position épistémologique complexe qui, en tant que concept clinique, éclaire la pratique psychanalytique contemporaine. Se concentrant sur l'histoire, les traductions et les usages de cette notion de « mise en acte », ainsi que son rapport aux concepts voisins, cet article explore les résultats de recherches empiriques et conceptuelles. L’article examinera également le statut épistémologique actuel de cette notion de mise en acte en tant que concept scientifique particulier et dans sa relation à la théorie psychanalytique générale.

1986 prägt Jacobs prägte Enactment als psychoanalytisches Konzept. Davon ausgehend hat sich dieses Konzept innerhalb der psychoanalytischen Gemeinschaft weiterentwickelt. Ursprünglich beschrieb Enactment einen bestimmten Aspekt der Gegenübertragung, der im analytischen Prozess durch subtile Handlungen ausgedrückt wird, die sich von den lautstärker nach Aufmerksamkeit suchenden Darstellungen unterscheiden, die in das Spektrum des “Ausagierens“ fallen. In der heutigen Zeit bringt das Konzept Enactment einige Schwierigkeiten mit sich, die nicht ignoriert werden sollten. Als theoretisches Konzept nimmt Enactment eine komplexe erkenntnistheoretische Position ein, und als klinisches Konzept beleuchtet es aktuelle psychoanalytische Methoden. Dieser Beitrag untersucht die Ergebnisse einer empirischen und begriffsorientierten Forschungsarbeit, setzt den Schwerpunkt bei Geschichte, Übersetzungen und Gebrauch des Begriffs “Enactment“ und beim Vergleich mit verwandten Termini. Er betrachtet außerdem die erkenntnistheoretische Perspektive hinsichtlich der aktuellen Stellung von Enactment als einem wissenschaftlichen Konzept und dessen Beziehung zur psychoanalytischen Theorie.

Coniato da Jacobs nel 1986, il concetto psicoanalitico di enactment si è da allora considerevolmente sviluppato all'interno della comunità analitica. In un primo tempo, con il termine di enactment si designava un aspetto del controtranfert espresso attraverso azioni impercettibili all'interno del processo analitico: azioni diverse dal tipo di performance più vistose che ricadono sotto la definizione di “agito”. Per come è inteso ai nostri giorni, il concetto di enactment presenta una serie di difficoltà che non andrebbero ignorate: da un punto di vista teorico il suo status epistemologico è complesso, mentre sul piano clinico esso aiuta a gettare luce sui modi contemporanei di praticare la psicoanalisi. Il presente articolo esamina i risultati di un lavoro di ricerca empirica e concettuale concentrandosi sulla storia, sulle traduzioni e sui diversi usi del termine “enactment”, passando inoltre in rassegna i vari modi in cui esso è stato paragonato ad altri termini affini. In chiave epistemologica si considererà anche l'attuale status dell'enactment in quanto concetto scientifico, e la sua relazione con il campo teorico della psicoanalisi.

El concepto psicoanalítico de enactment fue acuñado por Jacobs en 1986. Desde entonces, se ha desarrollado como concepto en la comunidad psicoanalítica. Originalmente describía un aspecto de la contratransferencia expresado a través de acciones sutiles dentro del proceso analítico, distinto de las actuaciones más ostensibles que caen dentro del espectro del acting-out. Hoy, el concepto de enactment presenta algunas dificultades que no deberían ser ignoradas. En cuanto concepto teórico, tiene una posición epistemológica compleja, y en cuanto concepto clínico, esclarece prácticas psicoanalíticas contemporáneas. El presente artículo explora los resultados de un trabajo de investigación empírica y conceptual, que se centra en la historia, las traducciones y los usos del término enactment y su comparación con términos relacionados. También aborda la perspectiva epistemológica respecto a su estado actual en cuanto concepto científico y su relación con el campo teórico psicoanalítico.

responseSet.title: The analyst’s listening: for, to, with
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The analyst’s listening: for, to, with

Bruce Reis

Bruce Reis

New York
USA

Bruce Reis   CONTACT Bruce Reis bruce.reis@nyu.edu 270 Lafayette Street, Suite 807, NY 10012, NY
Listening, non-representational, unlanguaged

Analytic approaches to listening have favored something that is more akin to vision, consequently shaping the technique, aims and understandings of psychoanalysis in terms of the production of understandings and meanings. The aural aspects of listening have mostly been subsumed under this emphasis on the visual. Two models of analytic listening based in the visual are initially presented, listening-for, and listening-to. Illustrations show how listening in these approaches emphasize “seeing” (i.e. discovering or creating conscious understandings or meanings) pertaining to unconscious contents. A third mode of listening, listening-with, based in an aural approach, seeks neither understanding nor meaning thought to be hidden, in need of (co)construction, or needing to be made figurable. Listening-with does not seek to symbolize content either already there or understood to be present as unrepresented. Content is not what is listened for or to, understanding or meaning not the goal. Instead it is an abiding within a space not only of not-knowing but of not trying to know. Exploring the unlanguaged from this latter perspective will involve those portions of the analysand’s mental life that are never transformed analytically into symbolized content, as well as experiences involving the patient’s mental life that do not call for any such transformation or understanding. The first part of this paper will feature a theoretical discussion of these modes of listening and the second part will illustrate the conception of listening-with via three vignettes selected from the literature, each demonstrating different aspects of the position. These modes of listening are considered to not operate exclusively of each other, but are heuristically separated for the current investigation in order to highlight attention to listening to phenomena existing outside of symbolization or referentiality, phenomena that may never take a form.

Les perspectives psychanalytiques relatives à l'écoute ont eu tendance à privilégier l'aspect visuel et, par conséquent, ont façonné la technique, les objectifs et la conception de la psychanalyse en termes de la compréhension et du sens. Du fait de l'accent mis sur le visuel, l'oralité s'y est vue incluse. L'auteur de cet article propose deux modèles de l'écoute psychanalytique basés sur le visuel : être à l'écoute de, écouter. Avec exemples à l'appui, il illustre comment, de ce point de vue, l'écoute met l'accent sur le « voir ». (c'est-à-dire la découverte ou la création de compréhensions ou de significations conscientes qui se rattachent à des contenus inconscients). Un troisième modèle de l'écoute, écouter avec, basé sur la dimension orale, ne cherche ni la compréhension ni le sens

Analytische Annäherungen an das (Zu)Hören haben etwas favorisiert, dass dem Sehen ähnlicher ist und folglich Methode, Ziele und Erkenntnisse der Psychoanalyse mit Bezug auf Erkenntnisse und Bedeutungen gestaltet. Die auralen Aspekte des Hörens sind hauptsächlich unter dieser Hervorhebung des Visuellen zusammengefasst worden. Zunächst werden zwei Modelle des analytischen Zuhörens auf einer visuellen Basis präsentiert, und zwar das genaue Hinhören und das Zuhören. Beispiele zur Veranschaulichung zeigen, wie das Hören bei diesen Formen der Annäherung das „Sehen“ (d. h. ein Erkennen oder Erzeugen von bewussten Erkenntnissen oder Bedeutungen) als zu unbewussten Inhalten gehörend betont. Eine dritte Art des Hörens, das Mit-Hören mit einem auralen Ansatz, strebt weder nach einer Erkenntnis noch nach einer Bedeutung, die als versteckt gilt, (ko-)konstruiert werden muss oder eine figurale Darstellung benötigt. Das Mit-Hören bemüht sich nicht darum, entweder bereits vorhandene oder als vorhanden, aber nicht repräsentiert wahrgenommene Inhalte zu symbolisieren. Es sind nicht Inhalte, die mit genauem Hinhören oder Zuhören gesucht werden, und Erkenntnis oder Bedeutung sind nicht das Ziel. Stattdessen ist es ein Verweilen in einem Raum, wo man nicht nur nicht weiß, sondern auch nicht versucht zu wissen. Das nicht sprachlich Ausgedrückte aus dieser letztgenannten Perspektive zu erkunden, bezieht sowohl jene Bereiche des mentalen Lebens des Analysanden ein, die nie analytisch in symbolisierte Inhalte umgewandelt werden, als auch Erfahrungen, die das mentale Leben des Patienten betreffen, die eine solche Transformation oder Erkenntnis nicht beanspruchen. Der erste Teil dieses Beitrags beinhaltet eine theoretische Diskussion dieser Arten des Hörens, und der zweite Teil veranschaulicht die Konzeption des Mit-Hörens anhand von drei Vignetten aus der Literatur, von denen jede andere Aspekte der Position aufzeigt. Diese Arten des Hörens gelten als nicht ausschließlich unabhängig voneinander wirkend, sind jedoch für diese Untersuchung heuristisch getrennt, um die Aufmerksamkeit auf das Zuhören bei Phänomenen zu lenken, die außerhalb von Symbolisierung oder Referentialität existieren – Phänomene, die möglicherweise niemals eine Gestalt annehmen.

Gli approcci psicoanalitici all'ascoltare hanno per lo più privilegiato una dimensione vicina al visivo: motivo per cui la tecnica, gli scopi e il tipo di comprensione che caratterizzano la psicoanalisi si sono man mano configurati soprattutto in quanto produzione di comprensione e di significati. Alla dimensione più propriamente aurale dell'ascolto è stato dato decisamente meno rilievo rispetto a quella del visivo. In questo lavoro vengono innanzitutto presentati due modelli di ascolto psicoanalitico basati sul visivo: quelli dell'ascolto-per e dell'ascolto-di. A tale riguardo si mostrerà in particolare come questi tipi di ascolto mettano l'accento sul "vedere": ovvero, sullo scoprire o sul creare comprensioni o significati consci relativi a contenuti inconsci. Una terza modalità di ascolto, quella di un ascoltare-con fondato su un approccio di tipo aurale, non appare invece alla ricerca di comprensioni o significati ritenuti nascosti, bisognosi di (co)costruzione o di accesso alla figurabilità. L'ascoltare-con non cerca di simbolizzare dei contenuti già presenti o magari presenti ma ancora non rappresentati: non è infatti il contenuto ciò che in questo caso si ascolta, così come non sono la comprensione e il significato gli obiettivi dell'ascoltare, bensì il sostare in uno spazio in cui non soltanto non si sa, ma nemmeno si tenta di sapere. Condotta da questo vertice, l'esplorazione di una realtà che non ha incontrato il linguaggio verrà a interessare quelle parti della vita mentale dell'analizzando che non vengono mai trasformate analiticamente in contenuto simbolico, rivolgendosi a esperienze che coinvolgono la vita mentale del paziente senza tuttavia richiedere alcuna trasformazione o comprensione di tal genere. La prima parte dell'articolo presenterà una discussione teorica di queste modalità di ascolto, mentre nella seconda parte si illustrerà il concetto di ascoltare-con attraverso tre vignette cliniche tratte dalla letteratura, ciascuna delle quali getta luce su diversi aspetti di questa particolare posizione. Questi modi di ascoltare non vanno ritenuti incompatibili l'uno con l'altro, e vengono distinti nel discorso qui sviluppato soltanto per ragioni euristiche, al fine di sottolineare l'importanza di ascoltare fenomeni che esistono al di fuori di ogni simbolizzazione o referenzialità: fenomeni che potrebbero non acquisire mai una forma.

Los abordajes psicoanalíticos a la escucha han favorecido algo que se parece más la visión, configurando, por consiguiente, la técnica, los objetivos y las comprensiones del psicoanálisis en términos de producción de comprensiones y significados. Este énfasis en lo visual ha subsumido, en gran parte, los aspectos auditivos de la escucha. Se presentan, inicialmente, dos modelos de escucha analítica basada en lo visual: la escucha para y la escucha a. Se muestra, mediante ejemplos, cómo en estos enfoques la escucha enfatiza el “ver” (es decir, el descubrir o crear comprensiones o significados conscientes) en relación a contenidos inconscientes. Un tercer modo de escucha, la escucha con, basado en un enfoque auditivo, no busca ni la comprensión ni significados ocultos, que necesitan ser (co)construidos o vueltos representables. La escucha con no busca simbolizar contenidos que ya están allí o que se consideran presentes como no representados. No se escucha ni se busca escuchar el contenido; la meta no es la comprensión ni el significado. Más bien es permanecer dentro de un espacio no solo de no saber sino de no tratar de saber. Desde este perspectiva, la exploración de lo sin lenguaje involucrará aquellas porciones de la vida mental del analizado que nunca son transformadas analíticamente en contenido simbolizado, así como experiencias de la vida mental del paciente que no requieren tales transformaciones o comprensiones. La primera parte de este artículo presenta un análisis teórico de estos modos de escucha; la segunda, ilustra el concepto de escucha con mediante tres viñetas literarias, cada una demostrando diferentes aspectos de esta posición. Se considera que estos modos de escucha no se excluyen entre sí, pero para la presente investigación han sido separados heurísticamente a fin de llamar la atención sobre la escucha de fenómenos que existen fuera de la simbolización o la referencialidad, fenómenos que posiblemente nunca tomen forma.
