responseSet.title: Violence in infancy and its relationship with perverse aspects in the development of a child
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Violence in infancy and its relationship with perverse aspects in the development of a child

Rosely Lerner

Rosely Lerner

Rosely Lerner   CONTACT Rosely Lerner roselycbl@gmail.com

Patients feel humiliated when they feel small, dependent, and looked down on. They may defend themselves against such feelings by looking down on the analyst or by attempting to enlist the analyst to join them in looking down on someone else.

(Steiner, J., 2011, p.26).

What I seek to illustrate in this work is the precocious origin of perverse behaviour which, when observed in analyses with adults, is found to be already structured. As an example, I took the case of a child of five and a half with a significant level of violence, who, over several years, had experiences of humiliation by the father and, in a way, seduction by the mother. I believe it is important to highlight that we can never diagnose a child as perverse because they are still developing. What I aim to show with this clinical material is how situations of humiliation and exposure to adult sexuality, creating premature eroticization, are central to the development of patterns of behaviour that may become organised as a perverse structure in adult age. To this, we add the high level of aggression of the child as an aggravating factor. What I seek to demonstrate is how early psychoanalytic treatment helped this patient to organise himself differently, making him more capable of integrating these aspects of his personality.

I believe that this is revealing clinical material because the profound study of the development of primitive emotions taken up by many authors, together with what we see in our patients, serves as a basis for the understanding of what we find in current clinical work and the changes that are emerging in society. These changes become controversial when speaking of sexuality and gender identity.

In developing this work, I have only considered authors that find an echo in my clinical experience, although I recognise other contributions that speak of the subject.

Some Theoretical Contributions

Psychoanalytically, when it comes to sexual manifestations, perversion can be understood as a clinical expression of destructive impulses that distort, alter and divert sexuality. This is the psychoanalytic understanding that seems to me to be the most complete one. However, in contemporary speech, perversion is used as a synonym for cruelty, malice, wickedness or destructiveness, as well as a deviation of the norm, above all in the psychic, moral or sexual terrain. The term is even used to describe practices characterised by finding pleasure in things that normally generate horror or aversion. 543

Beyond psychotherapy: on becoming a (Radical) psychoanalyst

[by]Barnaby B. Barratt, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 207 pp., £29.99, ISBN: 9781138362222

Maxine Anderson

Maxine Anderson

Seattle
WA, 98121
USA

Maxine Anderson   maxinekander@gmail.com

Barnaby B. Barratt has written several significant books and articles suggesting that the value of lived experience, as opposed to cognition, has long been under-valued in the psychoanalytic endeavour. His latest book on the subject, and the focus of this review, is the third volume in a trilogy that engages the psychoanalytic reader on several levels.

In the first volume of the trilogy (Barratt 2013) the author describes in some detail how psychoanalysis has enriched our understanding of the human condition. He commends Freud’s early investigations utilizing free association as crucial in the development of the field. But he feels that the drift toward orthodoxy which he cites as beginning with the formation of the Secret Committee, in 1912, diverted from the explorations via free association of the repressed unconscious as rooted in the body and in pre-cognitive experience. Barratt feels this trend has continued amidst the several current schools of psychoanalytic thought, to the scientific detriment of the field. 638

Psychoanalysis – a general theory of psychical structure formation and pathogenesis; Psychoanalyse: Eine allgemeine Theorie der psychischen Strukturbildung und Pathogenese

by Peter Zagermann, New York, International Psychoanalysis Books, 2019, 341pp., $35.00 (paperback), ISBN: 978-1-949093-35-3; Von Peter Zagermann. Kindle 2020, 340pp

Hans-Volker Werthmann

Hans-Volker Werthmann

Germany

Hans-Volker Werthmann   hvwerthmann@online.de

The Munich psychoanalyst Peter Zagermann steps in front of the psychoanalytic world with the claim that he is presenting an integrated general psychoanalytic concept of psychic structure and pathogenesis, based on a limited number of elements and satisfying the demands of the theory of science. As the author propounds, the correctness of such a psychoanalytic theory has to be measured against clinical practice; it must provide adequate psychodynamic clarification for those psychic disorders discovered first by Sigmund Freud and his successors, and the subject of research for now more than 120 years.

Over time many psychoanalytic concepts have come into existence, which were only loosely or not at all based on Freud’s metapsychology. This was owed to having to deal with ever more complex pathologies. Metapsychology retreated to the background and even fell into disrepute as useless. And thus also the claim of being a science became ever less justifiable.

It is, therefore, a bold undertaking to once again start out at the very place where Freud let up in his “Outline of Psychoanalysis”. The author is well positioned for this, as he has presented very differentiated psychoanalytic investigations with “Ego-Ideal, Sublimation, Narcissism – the 645

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst’s Desire: The Ethical Foundation of Clinical Practice, by Mitchell Wilson, New York, Bloomsbury Academic, 2020, 234 pp, $99, ISBN: 9781501328046

Jeanne Wolff Bernstein

Jeanne Wolff Bernstein

Vienna, Austria
jeanne.wolffbernstein@gmail.com

Jeanne Wolff Bernstein  

Statements such as “The analyst’s desire is not a pure desire. It is a desire to obtain absolute difference, a desire which intervenes when, confronted with the primary signifier, the subject is, for the first time, in a position to subject himself to it” (Lacan [1964a] 1973, 276) have left readers - Lacanians or not - perplexed about how they ought to understand this strange thing called the analyst’s desire. Other mantras such as that “the desire of the analyst is the desire to keep an opposition between demand and desire” ([1966] 2002, 529), or that “it is ultimately the analyst's desire that operates in psychoanalysis” ([1966] 2002, 724), have helped little to further an understanding of this complex concept. Bruce Fink represents an exception since he contributes to clarifying the position of the analyst when he writes that “The analyst in the position of cause of desire for the analysand is, according to Lacan, the motor force of analysis; in other words, it is the position the analyst must occupy in order for transference to lead to something other than identification with the analyst at the endpoint of an analysis (identification with the analyst being considered the goal of analysis by certain psychoanalysts)” (1997, 39) (emphasis is in the original).

However, it is Mitchell Wilson’s recent book The Analyst’s Desire: The Ethical Foundation of Clinical Practice that renders this complicated idea more understandable and brings it into relationship with the British and Relational Schools’ singular focus on countertransference, in particular its chief protagonist, the process of projective identification. Wilson is no longer taking on the politics of the IPA from the 1950s, nor the past goals of the ego psychologists who had forcefully advocated the goal of psychoanalysis to be a positive identification with the analyst. Instead, Wilson gets right into the trenches of the Kleinian, Bionian and Independent School traditions in order to peel away and examine the different layers of the Lacanian analyst, functioning in an ethical position of carrying the unconscious desire for the analysis versus the Bionian/Relational analysts, who see themselves as vessels for, and containers of, the analysand’s unwanted aspects of their desire. Unlike other Lacanian analysts, Wilson does not work with fellow Lacanians, but with psychoanalysts from different persuasions at the San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis, compelling him to define the Lacanian clinical practice in less jargoned terms and in response to other clinical convictions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis from the Inside Out: Developing and Sustaining an Analytic Identity and Practice, by Lena Ehrlich, London and New York, Routledge, 2020, 169 pp, $38.95, ISBN: 978-0-367-50519-6

Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

Hanover, NH, USA
ackerman@post.harvard.edu

Sarah Ackerman  

Lena Ehrlich’s book, Psychoanalysis from the Inside Out: Developing and Sustaining an Analytic Identity and Practice, should be read by every person who aspires to have a robust analytic practice. However, readers may not want to read it at bedtime - for me, it had the power to disrupt my sleep!

In this book, Ehrlich turns a psychoanalytic eye on the practising analyst. She carefully and thoughtfully draws out innumerable internal resistances on the analyst’s part to the practice of analysis, illuminating resistances to recommending analysis, resistances to deepening analysis and broader resistances to sustaining an analytic practice. In the process, she forcefully addresses our own role in diminishing the practice of psychoanalysis, highlighting our conflicting swings between idealizing analysis and devaluing it. As Ehrlich insists, “If we’re to do justice to the complexity of our work as analysts, we have no resting place regarding understanding our motivations. As soon as we’ve identified our reluctance to analyze, we need to question our analytic zeal” (87).

Ehrlich opens the book with an examination of the analyst’s reluctance to offer analysis, unearthing the way in which behind the allure of training to be an analyst lies a hidden fear, and at times even a repulsion, of the messy affects that psychoanalysis stirs in both the analyst and her analysand. Ehrlich shrugs off the external rationalizations as to why psychoanalysis is less commonly practised, even by trained analysts. Instead, Ehrlich looks inward, to consider the analyst’s counter-resistances, her ambivalence and even her unconscious hatred of analysis. This is where the ground may tremble under a reader’s feet: Ehrlich proposes that trained analysts frequently eschew the recommendation of analysis even on occasions when their training affirms its appropriateness. Through clinical vignettes, she displays an array of emotional vectors that underlie her own reluctance to analyse. Ehrlich makes no mention of Morris’ (2016) paper, “The Analyst’s Offer”, which articulates the enigmatic messages inherent in the recommendation of analysis and the impact on the patient of this inevitably seductive message. And yet it is clear that Ehrlich is well aware that the recommendation is opening up a Pandora’s box. Indeed, part of her effort is to challenge analysts to come to the conversation ready for the emotional upheaval that any good analysis invites for both parties. “Based on my own experience recommending analysis, I concur with the view that the recommendation for analysis is from the outset infused with realistic and infantile wishes and fears from both therapeutic partners” (20, emphasis added).

Illusion, disillusion, and irony in psychoanalysis, by John Steiner, London, England, Routledge, 2020, 180 pp, $39.95, 9780367467012

Neal Vorus

Neal Vorus

New York
nvorus@gmail.com

Neal Vorus  

Introduction

Illusion, Disillusion, and Irony in Psychoanalysis is a collection of John Steiner’s papers published over the past seven years, all of which share a central concern with the fundamental role of illusion in human life and the important developmental (and psychoanalytic) task of progressively facing reality through a process of disillusionment. While the interplay of illusion and disillusion is by no means a new concern in psychoanalysis, Steiner’s project over the past decade has been to illuminate its role in the human condition more broadly. As such, his book transcends the usual parameters of psychoanalytic writings, and might better be thought of as a book of psychoanalytically informed wisdom, distilled from Steiner’s many years of immersion in clinical work and theoretical elaboration of the modern Kleinian paradigm. It is a work that 826

responseSet.title: TransFeminine law
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

TransFeminine law

Jill Gentile

Jill Gentile

Jill Gentile   jillgentile3@gmail.com

To the editor:

Sam Semper’s recent review of my book Feminine Law (Gentile, 2016) brings an ideological, gender-political cast to her reading which ultimately creates misleading impressions that I am compelled to address. While I respect and even share Semper’s concerns regarding psychoanalysis’s exclusions and maltreatment of queer and gender non-conforming people, I believe she misreads key aspects of my argument. In Semper’s reading, Feminine Law conflates woman, female and feminine (as well as other terms), and reifies heterosexuality, potentially abetting the forces of cisheteropatriarchy and limiting, rather than liberating, the symbolic discourse and democratic polity I seek to expand. What Semper’s review ignores is that the book is explicitly anti-patriarchal in its aims, and is dedicated to theorizing, not gender per se, but how gender intersects with democracy and psychoanalysis – how they might create radically inclusive and egalitarian spaces for freedom of speech and desire. As Feminine Law demonstrates, the unsignified feminine marks the limit point of free association in the clinic and freedom of speech in the public sphere, whereas signifying the feminine can function to unleash unpredictable, vibrational, emancipatory motions of desire.

The book aims to disrupt a monopolistic-phallocratic symbolic order, dismantle patriarchal hierarchies and binaries, and challenge facile biological reductionism. By failing to symbolize the vaginal, psychoanalysis not only consigns the female body to the realm of the imaginary and objectified, but deprives the chain of signification of the very gap that might subvert the forces of phallocratic assimilation. It is by means of this liminal, feminine gap that novel unconscious elements can replenish the symbolic order, enabling a robust and inclusive free speech praxis. Insofar as the founding blueprints of both psychoanalysis (the Fundamental Rule) and constitutional democracy (in the United States, the First Amendment) “enshrine” this gap, they can be seen as expressions of feminine law.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Essential readings from the Melanie Klein Archives: Original papers and critical reflections

edited by Jane Milton, London and New York, Routledge and Taylor & Francis Group, 2020, $46.95, ISBN: 978-0-367-33790-2

Kyra Minninger

Kyra Minninger

Kyra Minninger   Kyraminninger@yahoo.com

Essential Readings from the Melanie Klein Archives: Original Papers and Critical Reflections is an important compilation of personal documents and lectures from the Klein Archives, together with significant research from noted Klein scholars (Jane Milton, Claudia Frank, R.D. Hinshelwood and Maria Rhode) edited by Jane Milton, archivist of the Melanie Klein Trust. The first part of the volume, “In Klein's Own Words”, includes the final version of Klein's autobiography.1 While fragments of it had been published previously, this is the first publication of the complete autobiography. Also included are a personal letter to her friend and colleague, Elliott Jaques, and four beautifully written and accessible lectures given to lay audiences between 1936 and 1939.2

In the second part, “Studies from the Melanie Klein Archives”, Milton presents the archival research. Claudia Frank's chapters (previously only available in German) focus on technical aspects of Klein's work: one on her thinking about how external reality emerges in internal reality, another on her use of reassurance, and a third on Klein's unsuccessful foray into applied psychoanalysis. In chapters previously published but revised for this book, R.D. Hinshelwood traces the development of Klein's ideas about repression and splitting, countertransference and internal objects. Milton's own contribution expands Klein's thoughts on her 1963 paper, “On a Sense of Loneliness”, adding new, personally meaningful and clinically relevant material. With special access to closed confidential files of Klein's work with her patient Dick, Maria Rhode discusses Klein's astute grasp of his autistic features before this condition had been recognized.

Part 1: In Klein's own words

Klein's lectures are a pleasure to read. One is immediately drawn into a sense of being in the audience, listening as Klein discusses her sophisticated ideas in simple and succinct terms. Speaking in non-technical language to a wide audience of educators, clinicians and others, 1064

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): Fictional Perspectives on the Infantile. From David Copperfield to Harry Potter

José Avzaradel

José Avzaradel

José Avzaradel  

Chair: Beatriz de León de Bernardi

Presenters: Maria Inês Neuenschwander Escosteguy Carneiro, Susana Vinocur Fischbein

Reporter: José Renato Avzaradel

Maria Inês Neuenchwander Escosteguy Carneiro (Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro) started the panel with her paper entitled “Two Hogwarts Boys. Harry Potter and Tom Riddle, Best Friends Forever?” She developed a detailed examination of the Harry Potter saga, engaging with Kleinian concepts; her paper was a tribute to her child patients. Heroes and villains, as mutual antagonists, share the role of perpetuating the necessary existence of good and evil, she argued.

For her, Hogwarts’s ambivalence is the guiding line of the entire saga. Carneiro pointed out that ambivalences are always present, and she suggested the possibility of integration or disintegration, which are intense emotional experiences, since the anxiety entailed can be overwhelming in two opposite ways. She suggested that, in view of the depressive position, the gain would be directly proportional to the price one paid, since integrating and repairing the object entailed a significant effort and ceaseless choices.

Carneiro discussed the generation of persecutory anxieties in the paranoid-schizoid position, and she referred to the omnipotent hat: nothing is hidden in one’s head that the Sorting Hat cannot see. Harry asked: “Why did the hat hesitate with me?” And Dumbledore told him why: the protective shield of loving reverie, which he received from his mother, saved him from being murdered, but not from being touched by evil.

Carneiro said that all ambivalence intrinsically carried double messages, and the solutions to the ambivalence that dominated Hogwarts is what fascinated Harry Potter’s readership. The two boys Tom/Voldemort and Harry Potter provide examples on how projective identification works in the primitive moments of mental development. In this fascinating human enigma, as Klein has demonstrated, an experience within the subject is projected into another person (the object), and vice versa.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): Ghost World in the Psychoanalytic Relationship

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish  

Chair: Giuseppe Civitarese

Presenters: Bruce Reis, Ruggero Levy, Beatrice Ithier

In his introduction, Giuseppe Civitarese articulated the panel’s intention: to venture into the “mysterious lost world” of non-represented or weakly represented elements in the mind as they emerge in the psychoanalytic relationship. The panelists addressed this subject in differing but connected ways.

Bruce Reis began his presentation by saying that his original title of “Phantom World” might more appropriately be “Faith-Works”, referring to Bion’s ideas about an analyst’s act of faith that opens us to what lies beyond our understanding. In this act of “negative capability” the analyst waits in a state of relaxed attention until something finite emerges from the infinite layer in the unconscious mental world. Bion stressed the non-religious aspect of faith in the psychoanalytic encounter by citing a scientific state of mind unaffected by memory and desire; Reis chose to return to the religious aspects by re-examining the conception of faith in the story of Abraham and Isaac in the Old Testament and the Epistle of James from the New Testament to inform Bion’s approach to exploration of the internal world.

For Bion, an act of faith becomes apprehensible when it can be represented in and by thought; for Reis, prior to this emergence, the analyst puts the self in a state of uncertainty, eschewing the sensuous body, desire and worldly things, beyond simple belief to an action in a path towards access to the unknown. Reis pointed out that in James faith consists, not in simple belief, but in action that aligns itself with nothing other than itself. Similarly, in Abraham’s obedient response to God’s command to sacrifice his son, Isaac, faith and conviction surrender to an action, justifying the self. Abraham is released from the horror of sacrificing his own son by the appearance of an angel who allows the substitution of a ram for the offering, discovered through an act of faith. For Bion, reaching for the unknown is accompanied by awe and also by catastrophe and horror, as in the story of Abraham. Returning to the topic of unrepresented or weakly represented mental elements, Reis argued that in this sense in an analysis mental energies are unbound and transformed through the action of faith itself. Through and beyond representation we find meanings and emotional truth. Reis’s ideas provided a framework for the clinical cases which followed.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Vancouver 2021 (Online): Infantile Longings and “The Child within the Adult”

Frances Thomson-Salo

Frances Thomson-Salo

Frances Thomson-Salo  

Chair: Andrea Celenza

Presenters: Andrea Celenza, Stanley J. Coen

Discussants: Mary Brady

Andrea Celenza introduced this panel about the infantile in the adult, exploring maternal erotism, traumatic experiences of loss and transferences-countertransferences, with two samples of analytic process, conveying how, with therapeutic intervention, the child within is accessed and integrated.

Celenza’s paper, “Maternal Erotic Transferences and the Work of the Abject”, drew on Kristeva’s work on maternal eroticism, the abject and revitalization in maternal eroticism, and Celenza noted that through erotic transferences, it can often be seen how an analysand has translated and internalized her mother’s vitality, as well as inhibitions, and destructive potentials. Celenza illustrated how she had reconceptualized a patient’s material through the lens of Kristeva’s work. Kristeva describes maternal eroticism in its deepest sense - beyond sexuality - as a vitalizing, nurturing vocation committed to the child’s life, while also the first agent of seduction, transmitting unconscious fantasies to the infant to be, and to have her to love, through touch and gaze. The maternal body is the first signifier of the erotic, but maternal eroticism is also devitalizing as a mother initiates her baby into confronting loss. Traumatic loss can create a dreaded void, the abject, concretely located in the maternal body as horrific objects that can infect life, a site of devitalizing fears. This void can be aligned with Green’s work of the negative, the unthought known, and as a child fears damaging the parent, the analysand fears harming (or being harmed by) the analyst. In a mother’s refusal to collapse, however, this transformation of destruction, the resignification of the abject, is the activity of maternal eroticism, paralleling the analytic process.

Celenza introduced an analysand, Petra, whose infantile erotic longings undermined her adult capacities. When Petra was 22 years old, her mother died of a blood disorder, and Petra feared she might contaminate others. Gradually in the analysis she began a secretive concretized “taking in” of Celenza, internalizing her by wearing a sweater to analysis which she did not wash so that it kept Celenza’s scent. Celenza, discussing the maternal erotic transference, described Petra’s introjection of her mother as “taking in”, through gaze and touch. Later in an après-coup of the analytic process, a memory of Petra’s idealized mother reflected a re-transcription of linked images of the mother as psychically absent, and persecutory. During this time, Celenza’s own mother became ill and when she returned to the sessions, Petra brought in some objects that she wanted

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Nancy Chodorow

Nancy Chodorow

Berkeley, CA, USA
nancy_chodorow@hms.harvard.edu

Nancy Chodorow  

Dear Editor,

I was saddened and shocked to find that, when assigning my 2020 book, The Psychoanalytic Ear and the Sociological Eye: Toward an American Independent Tradition, for review, the IJP North American Editor for Book Reviews seems to have looked only at my name, pigeonholed me as a woman who writes (or by implication, should write?) about women, and assigned my book to Paula Ellman, Chair of the IPA Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis. I admire Paula’s work for COWAP and have been moved by her own writing, but to my knowledge, she has written on almost nothing that my book is about. When I was IJP North American Editor for Book Reviews, I tried to invite someone whose writings and areas of expertise and interest were germane to the book itself. I hoped that a reviewer could and would read from “inside” the book and its intellectual and substantive context, from the point of view of the author, in the way that we try to listen clinically. My letter addresses both the choice of reviewer and the review itself.

The subject of my book is not hidden but in plain sight. A quick glance at its title, subtitle, and table of contents indicates that I have written an interdisciplinary book about an American independent tradition, the links between psychoanalysis and sociology, the mapping of theory, Loewald, and Erikson. My opening chapter and a later “recapitulation” confirm that I myself have identified and named this tradition, that I have created the formulation intersubjective ego psychology to describe it, and that I am in dialogue with the British independent tradition. The book grows from my location in both psychoanalysis and sociology, and in the United States, at least, there are only a handful of us who have been both. In both fields (also as a psychoanalytic feminist), I have been a theorist (in sociology, as in psychoanalysis, a controversial choice: where’s the research? where’s the clinical example?).

Only one chapter displays any connection to women, gender, or feminism, a chapter felicitously (perhaps ironically, in the present context) titled, “Why is it easy to be a psychoanalyst and a feminist but not a psychoanalyst and a sociologist?” In the index, one finds a few entries under women, gender, or feminism and by contrast, dozens of entries on Loewald, substantial listings under Erikson and Freud, listings for several British independents, and references to Durkheim, Mannheim, Marx, Simmel, and Weber among classical sociologists and Slater and Smelser among twentieth century psychoanalytic sociologists. Yet, the book was given for review not to someone who knows or identifies with the British independent tradition, a Loewald or Erikson scholar, someone who has focused on comparative psychoanalytic theory, someone who is both analyst and social scientist, or an analyst with known social science interests.

responseSet.title: Siblings, sex, and shame: The film Shame (2011)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Siblings, sex, and shame: The film Shame (2011)

Britt-Marie Schiller

Britt-Marie Schiller

Saint Louis
MO
USA

Britt-Marie Schiller   CONTACT Britt-Marie Schiller schillbm@webster.edu Ph.D., Saint Louis Psychoanalytic Institute, 7700 Clayton Road, Suite 204, Saint Louis, MO 63117, USA
Shame, addiction, incest

Shame is a hidden emotion. The organs of shame are the eyes; when we feel ashamed, we want to hide, not be seen. In the film Shame, the director Steve McQueen lingers on and explores the dynamics of shame hidden behind sex addiction. I argue that shame is the underlying cause of the sex addiction here, while there is also a vicious cycle, as addiction invariably turns into a new source of shame. The focus on the phenomenology of shame, in the here and now of the film, reveals two siblings struggling with threats of vulnerability, neediness, helplessness, and unlovability. One sibling presents a meticulously neat appearance, and his mask-like face functions as an invulnerable armor against shame, while the other sibling has no armor against her neediness and helplessness. We witness the vicissitudes of their shame and are left wondering whether their encounter, which ends in a suicide attempt and in the unravelling of defensive enactments, leads to a possibility of transformation and internal change. The director ends the film abruptly and inconclusively, sending us back into it to review and search nachträglich for clues to the outcome.

responseSet.title: The infantile: Its multiple dimensions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The infantile: Its multiple dimensions

Bernardo Tanis

Bernardo Tanis

Bernardo Tanis   CONTACT Bernardo Tanis bernardo.tanis@gmail.com Rua Capote Valente 432 conj. 142 Pinheiros São Paulo, SP, Brasil, CEP 054-001
Infantile, memory, temporality, infantile sexuality, analytic setting

What is originality? To see something that still has no name; that still cannot be named even though it is lying right before everyone’s eyes. The way people usually are, it takes a name to make something visible at all. Those with originality have usually been the name-givers.

Friedrich Nietzsche, The Gay Science (2001).

The infantile, in all its richness and complexity, is one of these things to which Freud gave a name. It is one of the main Freudian discoveries and, as Virginia Ungar states in her call for proposals for this Congress, “without the notion of the infantile, psychoanalysis simply would not exist”, a statement with which I fully agree.

The infantile is a distinctive mark of the human; all psychoanalysts deal with and handle this psychic dimension. The infantile is not something that concerns only child analysts, nor is it equivalent to childhood or to development stages. Unrelated to behavioural infantilism, the infantile – always sexual in the Freudian perspective put forward in the “Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality” (Freud 1905/1949) – can be apprehended in the psychoanalytic experience as a princeps manifestation of psychic reality, of the unconscious dimension of human subjectivity. The important contributions of generations of post-Freudian analysts have enhanced our understanding of the infantile and the complexity of forms and content through which the patient’s infantile is involved in countertransference and has an impact on our clinical practice.

The infantile is the result of a causal, non-linear overdetermination, open to chance, to uncertainty. Far from being a photographic memory of the past or a manifestation of childish behaviour in the adult, the infantile points to the modes of recording and inscribing what Freud called Erlebnis, “childhood experiences”.

The core thesis is that in psychoanalytic clinical practice, regardless of our preferences for this or any other theoretical-clinical model, the effectiveness of these inscriptions, their metabolization and possible symbolization, and their living manifestation in drives in the present will always be in play for the subject. Herein lies the relevance of the founding Freudian matrix of our field.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The “dragons of primeval days”: Termination and the persistence of the infantile

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard   CONTACT Glen O. Gabbard glen.gabbard@gmail.com 4306 Yoakum Blvd., Ste. 535, Houston, Texas, USA 77006
Termination, infantile, defences, therapeutic lifers

The persistence of the infantile is nowhere more apparent than in the termination process. This dread of termination may interfere with both the patient's capacity to terminate and the analyst's capacity to let patients go. Certain patients have an intense wish to maintain a permanent connection with the analyst. Analysing that wish does not necessarily change the patient's conviction that they are not ready to fully terminate. The love experienced in the transference may be the best love relationship that the patient has ever had. The intensity and pervasiveness of the infantile in our work may best be glimpsed by studying the defences against the infantile. Ultimately, we must respect the patient's limits in some cases, rather than pick them apart and cajole the patient into another view that we prefer. Freud was perhaps uncharacteristically candid in saying that he questioned whether a true transformation was achieved at the time of termination.

responseSet.title: On Winnicott’s concept of trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Winnicott’s concept of trauma

Jan Abram

Jan Abram

janabram3@gmail.com
Byron House, 112A Shirland Road, London W9 2BT, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8776-9036

Jan Abram  

Psychic trauma; psychic environment; regression; fear of breakdown; gross impingement; violation of the self

Winnicott’s clinical-theoretical matrix constitutes a distinction in underlying basic principles from both the Freudian and Kleinian models. In essence, Winnicott’s concept of trauma is entirely related to the vicissitudes of the early psychic parent-infant relationship in which the mother’s role is fundamental for the development of the newborn’s psyche at the beginning and subsequently. While Winnicott acknowledges the difference between physical and psychic trauma, his focus is on how the interpsychic relationship, psyche to nascent psyche, contributes to the infant’s intrapsychic world. Thus, early trauma is caused by psychic transmissions that emanate through and because of the mother’s affective states of mind towards her fetus-becoming-an-infant and her infant-becoming-a-child. The earlier that psychic trauma occurs in the nascent psyche, the more severe the outcome of the individual’s mental health. This is why Winnicott refers to psychosis as an environmental deficiency disease as distinct from psychotic defences. The author outlines Winnicott’s key concepts that are intrinsic to his theory of trauma i.e. the antisocial tendency, fear of breakdown and regression. These concepts are woven into the fabric of Winnicott’s theoretical matrix and highlight his perspective on psychoanalysis as a therapeutic treatment for all levels of psychic trauma.

responseSet.title: Post-Termination Self-Analysis and the Relinquishment of the Psychoanalytic Frame: Thoughts on a Fragment of Self-Analytic Work following a Traumatic Termination
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Post-Termination Self-Analysis and the Relinquishment of the Psychoanalytic Frame: Thoughts on a Fragment of Self-Analytic Work following a Traumatic Termination

Richard Zimmer

Richard Zimmer

New York, USA
richardbzimmermd@gmail.com

Richard Zimmer  

Termination, self-analysis, frame, bastion, trauma

Months following the traumatic termination of my analysis I engaged in self-analytic work in which I was able to rework the events of the termination and their significance in terms of my history. Fundamental differences between my self-analytic process and that of my formal analysis led me to consider how termination may affect the ensuing self-analytic process. In this paper, I look at aspects of termination that have to do with the dissolution of symbiotic ties with the analyst; I will try to show that relinquishment of the frame provides an impetus both for consolidation and reworking of an identification with the analyst, which may enable the analysand to develop a personal way of thinking about the unconscious that differs from that of the analyst. I further suggest that there are inevitable limitations to the analytic process which manifest in the development of irreducible bastions, and that the dissolution of the frame at termination provides an opportunity for self-analytic resolution of those bastions which may allow self-understanding beyond what could have been attained within the formal analytic setting. Finally, I offer some speculations on how these processes may have played out between Freud and Fliess in Freud's self-analysis.

responseSet.title: Transience and hope: A return to Freud in a time of pandemic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transience and hope: A return to Freud in a time of pandemic

Jonathan Lear

Jonathan Lear

Chicago
IL
USA

Chicago
IL
USA

Jonathan Lear   CONTACT Jonathan Lear jlear@uchicago.edu 5844 S Stony Island Avenue, Chicago, IL, 60637, USA
Freud, superego, mourning

This essay is about what it is like to read Freud again in the time of the coronavirus pandemic. It offers a close reading of Freud’s essay “On Transience” and it brings to light how it might be read differently with the thoughts of world-catastrophe on our minds.

Passagèreté et espoir : un retour à Freud en temps de pandémie

L'auteur de cet article évoque ce que signifie de relire Freud aujourd'hui en ce temps de la pandémie de Covid-19. Il propose une lecture attentive du texte de Freud « Passagèreté », qui met en évidence une autre lecture possible de ce texte à la lumière de ce que suscite la catastrophe mondiale dans notre esprit.

Vergänglichkeit und Hoffnung: eine Rückbesinnung auf Freud in einer Zeit der Pandemie

In diesem Essay geht es darum, wie es ist, Freud in der Zeit der Coronavirus-Pandemie erneut zu lesen. Durch eine genaue Betrachtung von Freuds Essay „Vergänglichkeit“ kommt zum Vorschein, wie Freuds Abhandlung anders ausgelegt werden könnte, während uns die Gedanken an eine weltweite Katastrophe beschäftigen.

Caducità e speranza. Un ritorno a Freud in tempo di pandemia

Il presente lavoro parla dell'esperienza di rileggere Freud durante la pandemia di coronavirus. Il saggio freudiano "Caducità" viene analizzato in queste pagine attraverso una lettura ravvicinata che mette anche in luce come il modo di leggerlo possa cambiare laddove si abbia nella mente il pensiero di una catastrofe su scala globale.

Transitoriedad y esperanza: Un retorno a Freud en tiempos de pandemia

El presente ensayo trata sobre cómo es releer a Freud en tiempos de la pandemia del coronavirus. Ofrece una lectura detenida del ensayo “Sobre la transitoriedad” de Freud y saca a la luz cómo podría leerse de diferente manera teniendo en mente la idea de catástrofe mundial.

responseSet.title: Didier Anzieu: La psychanalyse, encore!
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Didier Anzieu: La psychanalyse, encore!

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert   CONTACT Catherine Chabert catherine@chabert.org

Introduction: Life and work

The contradictory demands that characterize Didier Anzieu’s work, which clearly appear in the text chosen for this commentary, also shape his unusual and unfinished portrait. His life and work can sometimes follow clear and easily discernible lines of energy; but around these revolve multiple orientations, movements of numerous interests, which form a kind of aureole difficult to grasp, but whose continuous and insistent presence must be taken into account if we are to see the complete picture. Two parallel versions should be constructed: one that would take up the chronology of an almost linear resumé identifying the major early stages (the years of training, the entry to the École Normale Supérieure, the doctorate in philosophy, the turn to psychology, the encounter with psychoanalysis), then the teaching activities and university career, the break with Lacan and the commitment to the French Psychoanalytical Association (APF) – and always, as a continuous thread, research and writing rooted in the very body of psychoanalysis and in the thought of its originator. And another version that would focus on the author’s multiple interests: projective methods and psychodrama, the unconscious and the group, philosophy and fairy tales, Pascal and Beckett … . And from these two versions could perhaps emerge an idea of the man, of his work, inseparably linked by a “single skin”, like the fantasy uncovered by Didier Anzieu relating to mother and child.

Didier Anzieu was drawn into the movements of psychoanalytic thought in France and thus into the vagaries of its developments. Trained at the Paris Psychoanalytical Society (SPP) and the French Psychoanalytical Society (SFP), he was an active player in the splits that occurred in one and then the other society, the first time with Lacan, the second time against him. In 1964, he was a participant in the founding group of the APF, within which he worked energetically and continuously. From his point of view, the most important disagreements between psychoanalysts concerned training policies rather than theoretical conceptions:

Every human being wants to train his children, his students, his successors in his own image. Or to the opposite image, which amounts to the same. With maturity and the spectacle of the collapse of so many ideologies around me, I have tried to stick to scepticism, accuracy and serenity. (Anzieu in Parot and Richelle 1992, 259)

responseSet.title: Philip M. Bromberg (1931–2020). Trauma, dissociation, and the multiple self
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Philip M. Bromberg (1931–2020). Trauma, dissociation, and the multiple self

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

New York City
USA

New York City
USA

Donnel Stern   donnelstern@gmail.com

That intimacy and depth of relatedness were so precious to Philip Bromberg, even his central truth and purpose, is perhaps an irony, because he was a shy person, not given to demonstrativeness, certainly not an extrovert. But his privacy and reserve, however prominent they may have been to those who encountered him in person, are probably much less evident to anyone who knows the man only from his work. In what follows I attend mainly to Bromberg’s ideas, which have been, and continue to be, among the most influential bodies of work written by any interpersonal/relational analyst. But the heart of the man, this sense he always had of the profound affective possibilities for human relatedness, deserves precedence in considering both his life and his work.

Psychoanalytic training, early clinical and theoretical commitments

The understanding of Bromberg’s views calls for some background about the psychoanalytic world from which he emerged, and to which he contributed. He was born in Brooklyn in 1931 and grew up there with an imaginative mother who told him stories she made up on the spot. He tells us she was “a poet by nature” (Greif and Livingston 2013, 323). Was it perhaps she who inspired his love for both the deeply felt life and the poetry and fiction he read throughout his life and wove so expressively and effectively into his writings? After college and then a brief period studying for a doctorate in English literature, he became a psychologist, and then, in the late 1960s and early 1970s, he trained as a psychoanalyst at the William Alanson White Institute (WAWI) in New York City, drawn there by the ideas of Sullivan (1940, 1953, 1954, 1956), whose thinking was central to WAWI. From the beginning, Bromberg believed that problems in living did not derive from intrapsychic conflict but from the kinds of destructive interpersonal events that he eventually came to refer to as “relational trauma”. Sullivan himself seldom, if ever, used the word “trauma”, but as Bromberg became more and more deeply immersed in the clinical study of trauma, he understood Sullivan’s (1940, 1953, 1954, 1956) theory of how human living goes awry to be a kind of trauma theory.

responseSet.title: Drive theory, redux: a history and reconsideration of the drives
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Drive theory, redux: a history and reconsideration of the drives

Jordan Conrad

Jordan Conrad

New York
NY
USA

Leuven
Belgium

Jordan Conrad   CONTACT Jordan A. Conrad Jordan.Conrad@student.kuleuven.be LMSW – Institute of Philosophy, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Kardinaal Mercierplein 2, 3000 Leuven, Belgium
Freudian theory, history of psychoanalysis, metapsychology, philosophy, conceptual research, historical research, psychic structures, unconscious

A large and significant portion of contemporary psychoanalytic theory has given up on the drives. The shift toward object relations in the 1940s and 50s, the scepticism about metapsychology in the latter half of the twentieth century, and a general confusion about the coherence of Freud’s drive theory have all contributed to their slow decline in prominence. There are legitimate criticisms of the drives that deserve attention but the drives themselves require a careful examination before any successful defence of their place in the metapsychology may be mounted. The current paper provides an account of the drives informed by the intellectual history of German and English thought related to the drives and instincts as they came to Freud. This history allows us to clearly distinguish between “drive” (or Trieb) and its conceptual neighbour “instinct” (or Instinkt).

Une part importante et significative de la théorie psychanalytique contemporaine a abandonné la réflexion sur les pulsions. L'infléchissement vers les relations d'objet dans les années quarante et cinquante, le scepticisme à l'égard de la métapsychologie dans la seconde moitié du vingtième siècle, ainsi qu'une confusion générale à propos de la cohérence de la théorie des pulsions de Freud, tout cela aura contribué à un lent déclin de leur importance. Il existe des critiques légitimes des pulsions qui méritent notre attention ; cependant, les pulsions en tant que telles nécessitent un examen minutieux avant de pouvoir monter une défense en bonne et due forme de leur place dans la métapsychologie. L'auteur de cet article fournit une explication des pulsions, éclairée par l'histoire intellectuelle de la pensée allemande et anglaise relative aux pulsions et instincts tels qu'ils sont apparus chez Freud. Cette histoire nous permet d'établir une distinction précise entre la « pulsion » (ou Trieb) et son voisin conceptuel, l' « instinct » (ou Instinkt).

Ein großer und wesentlicher Teil der psychoanalytischen Theorie der Gegenwart hat das Nachdenken über die Triebe aufgegeben. Die Hinwendung zu Objektbeziehungen in den 1940er und 1950er Jahren, die Skepsis gegenüber der Metapsychologie in der zweiten Hälfte des 20. Jahrhunderts sowie eine allgemeine Unklarheit hinsichtlich der Kohärenz von Freuds Triebtheorie haben alle dazu beigetragen, dass sie in einem langsamen Prozess an Bedeutung verloren haben. Es gibt legitime, unsere Aufmerksamkeit verdienende Kritikpunkte an den Trieben, aber die Triebe selbst müssen sorgfältig untersucht werden, bevor irgendeine Erfolg versprechende Verteidigung ihres Platzes in der Metapsychologie erfolgen kann. Der vorliegende Beitrag Geprägt schildert – geprägt von der Geistesgeschichte des deutschen und englischen Denkens über Triebe – die verschiedenen Triebe und Instinkte nach Freud. Diese Geschichte gestattet uns, eine klare Unterscheidung zwischen „Trieb“ und dem begrifflichen Nachbarn „Instinkt“ zu treffen.

La teoria psicoanalitica contemporanea ha in larga parte cessato di occuparsi delle pulsioni. Il passaggio al paradigma delle relazioni oggettuali verificatosi negli Anni '40 e '50, lo scetticismo con cui alla fine del XX secolo si guardava alla metapsicologia e un più generale senso di confusione circa la coerenza stessa della teoria pulsionale di Freud sono stati fattori che, messi insieme, hanno tutti contribuito al graduale declino di questo concetto. Sebbene alcune delle critiche rivolte alla teoria pulsionale meritino seria considerazione, per poter difendere in modo sensato il posto delle pulsioni all'interno della metapsicologia è necessario che le si esaminino attentamente per ciò che esse sono. Il presente lavoro ripercorre il concetto di pulsione muovendo dalla storia intellettuale del pensiero tedesco e inglese sulle pulsioni e sugli istinti, e da come essi appaiono nell'opera di Freud. Questo percorso storico permette tra l'altro di operare una chiara distinzione tra il concetto di “pulsione” (Trieb) e la nozione ad esso limitrofa di “istinto” (Instinkt).

Una parte grande y significativa de la teoría psicoanalítica contemporánea ha dejado de pensar en las pulsiones. Contribuyó al gradual descenso de su prominencia el desplazamiento hacia las relaciones objetales ocurrido en las décadas de 1940 y 1950, así como el escepticismo respecto a la metapsicología en la segunda mitad del siglo pasado, más una confusión general respecto a la coherencia de la teoría pulsional de Freud. Hay críticas legítimas a las pulsiones que merecen atención, pero, antes de montar una defensa exitosa de su lugar en la metapsicología, se requiere un examen cuidadoso de ellas mismas. El presente artículo brinda un recuento de las pulsiones basado en la historia intelectual del pensamiento alemán e inglés relativo a ellas y los instintos, tal como aparecieron en Freud. Esta historia nos permite distinguir claramente entre “pulsión” (o Trieb) y su vecino conceptual “instinto” (o Instinkt).

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic ethics, maintaining psychic reality in the intermediate space
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic ethics, maintaining psychic reality in the intermediate space

Jérome Glas

Jérome Glas

Lyon
France

Jérome Glas   CONTACT Jérome Glas jerome.glas@gmail.com Psychanalyste, Membre de la Société Psychanalytique de Paris - (SPP), Membre de la Société Suisse de Psychanalyse - (SSPsa), Chargé d’enseignement à l’Université de Paris, 9 rue de la Monnaie 69002, Lyon
Reality, psychic reality, ethics, method, transference, persecution

The author reflects on the ethical status of psychoanalysis. He argues that the ethical specificity of psychoanalysis is determined by its method. He distinguishes between concepts tied to the analytic frame – the protocol, technique and method. The method is the main guarantee of the ethical engagement between analyst and patient – in particular through the transference. The conditions of opening up the transference are maintained by a particular aspect of the analytic method, namely stating the fundamental rule of free association, which turns the setting into a process. The author illustrates his remarks by discussing Sophie’s psychoanalytic treatment in which a passionate transference emerges. A chance external event brings about a rupture in the transitional space of the session’s psychic reality, and thereby makes the specific status of the transference fall away and compromises the analytic work. Consequently, the analyst is deprived of his ethical status as object of the patient’s psychic reality, and as the recipient of the transference, and becomes an ordinary object in the field of everyday objectality. He simply becomes the person he is in external reality, with his various physical attributes.

L'auteur de cet article réfléchit au statut éthique de la psychanalyse. Il soutient que la spécificité éthique de la psychanalyse est conditionnée par sa méthode. Il établit une distinction entre les concepts liés au cadre analytique – le protocole, la technique et la méthode. La méthode est la principale garantie de l'engagement entre analyste et patient – en particulier par le biais du transfert. Les conditions d'ouverture du transfert sont soutenues par un aspect particulier de la méthode analytique, à savoir, l'institution de la règle fondamentale de la libre association, qui transforme le cadre en un processus. L'auteur illustre son propos en discutant le cas de Sophie dont le traitement psychanalytique a fait émerger un transfert passionnel. Un événement extérieur fortuit provoque une rupture au sein de l'espace transitionnel de la réalité psychique de la séance et, de ce fait, vient infirmer le statut du transfert et compromettre le travail analytique. Dès lors, l'analyste se voit priver de son statut éthique en tant qu'objet de la réalité psychique du patient et destinataire du transfert, pour devenir un objet ordinaire dans le domaine d'une objectalité commune. Il devient tout simplement la personne qu'il est dans la réalité extérieure, avec ses différentes caractéristiques physiques.

Der Autor reflektiert über den ethischen Status der Psychoanalyse. Er argumentiert, dass die ethische Besonderheit der Psychoanalyse von ihrer Methode bestimmt wird. Er unterscheidet zwischen Konzepten, die an den analytischen Rahmen gebunden sind – also Regelwerk, Technik und Methode. Die Methode ist der Hauptgarant für eine ethische Verbindung zwischen Analytiker und Patient – insbesondere durch die Übertragung. Die Bedingungen für eine Öffnung der Übertragung werden von einer Besonderheit der analytischen Methode aufrechterhalten, und zwar durch das Benennen der Grundregel der freien Assoziation, die das Setting in einen Prozess verwandelt. Der Autor veranschaulicht seine Ausführungen, indem er Sophies psychoanalytische Behandlung erörtert, bei der eine leidenschaftliche Übertragung zutage tritt. Eine von außen einwirkende zufällige Begebenheit verursacht einen Bruch im Übergangsraum der psychischen Realität der Sitzung. Dies lässt den Sonderstatus der Übertragung wegbrechen und gefährdet die analytische Arbeit. Infolgedessen wird der Analytiker seiner ethischen Stellung als Objekt der psychischen Realität der Patientin und Empfänger der Übertragung beraubt und wird dann zu einem gewöhnlichen Objekt im Bereich alltäglicher Objektbeziehungen. Er wird schlicht zu der Person, die er in der äußeren Realität ist – mit seinen vielfältigen körperlichen Merkmalen.

L'autore riflette sullo status etico della psicoanalisi sostenendo che la specificità etica della disciplina è determinata dal suo metodo. Egli opera a tale riguardo una distinzione tra i diversi concetti legati alla cornice psicoanalitica - il protocollo, la tecnica e il metodo. Il metodo rappresenta la principale garanzia di un rapporto etico tra analista e paziente, in particolare attraverso la dinamica del transfert. Le condizioni perché il transfert possa svilupparsi sono mantenute da un particolare aspetto del metodo analitico, e cioè dal fatto che venga stabilita la regola fondamentale delle associazioni libere - cosa che trasforma il setting in un processo. L'autore illustra le idee qui esposte discutendo il trattamento psicoanalitico di Sophie, in cui si assiste all'emergere di un transfert appassionato. Un evento esterno dovuto al caso interviene però a un certo punto a produrre una rottura nello spazio transizionale della realtà psichica della seduta, facendo venir meno lo specifico status del transfert e compromettendo quindi il lavoro analitico. L'analista si trova così privato del suo status etico di oggetto della realtà psichica del paziente e di destinatario del transfert, diventando un oggetto come tutti gli altri nel campo dell'oggettualità quotidiana: diventa cioè semplicemente la persona che egli è nella realtà esterna, con tutte le sue varie caratteristiche fisiche.

El autor reflexiona sobre el estatuto ético del psicoanálisis. Sostiene que la especificidad ética del psicoanálisis está determinada por su método, y distingue los conceptos vinculados al marco analítico: el protocolo, la técnica y el método. El método es la principal garantía del compromiso ético entre el analista y el paciente, en particular, mediante la transferencia. El establecimiento de la regla fundamental de la asociación libre, que convierte al encuadre en un proceso, es el aspecto concreto del método analítico que hace que las condiciones de la apertura de la transferencia se mantengan. El autor ilustra sus comentarios mediante el estudio del tratamiento psicoanalítico de Sophie, en el cual surge una transferencia apasionada. Un suceso externo fortuito ocasiona una ruptura del espacio transicional de la realidad psíquica de la sesión, de modo que desaparece el estatuto específico de la transferencia y afecta el trabajo analítico. Por lo tanto, el analista es privado de su estatuto ético como objeto de la realidad psíquica del paciente y como receptor de la transferencia, y se convierte en objeto ordinario en el campo de la objetalidad cotidiana. Pasa a ser simplemente la persona que es en la realidad externa, con sus diversos atributos físicos.
